five_things_to_know:
During my 4 decades of work in the built environment as an architect, builder, energy engineer, general contractor, educator, mentor and manufacturer, I have never thought of integration of the built environment much beyond the technical, aesthetic and cost optimization aspects of systems thinking.  For over 35 years, my work as a design/builder specifically helped me develop a robust integrated delivery system that uses systems thinking to integrate and facilitate the reliable delivery of buildings, both on time and on budget.  Using this integrated system, it allowed our firm to sign delivery contracts at 30% design development documents.  This system worked well with 75% of our projects delivered within 2.5% of the initial contract amount and our average delivery 11 days prior to contract end date. So while I am comfortable with systems thinking, I never allowed myself to view the built environment beyond the “system” I was familiar with. This system is the same waters all practitioners in the built environment swim in.  It is one of contracts, silos, anticipation of negative outcomes and generally a limited view of each of our individual places in the larger “system” or project.

All of this changed and my concepts of what may be possible was expanded when, in October of 2018, I was inducted into the Edmund Hillary Fellowship through the Edmund Hillary Institute in New Zealand as a Global Impact Entrepreneur Fellow.  During the 10-day induction I understood in a deep and moving way that there were other ways of approaching the concept of society and economy.  I embarked on a personal education mission to understand this and while I am still very much learning I have come to some working hypothesis. 

As a design/builder I always have had to "think inside the box", no matter how conceptual or cutting edge my ideas were, for my projects to be successful, I literally had to deliver floors, walls and roofs, "a BOX".  Since becoming an Edmund Hillary Fellow, I have dedicated myself to investigating a theoretical question "What could the built environment look like in a society in 2020 that has a historical underpinning of Regeneration (Healing), Abundance (the concept of Enough), and Kindness (Aroha)?

I conceive of history as a tapestry that is still on the loom.  All the portions that have been woven are our history, represented by colors, textures and patterns. The edge of the weaving is NOW and the fibers still yet to be woven are our future.  I am using a type of theoretic thought experiment to try to work out what the built environment would feel like by theoretically re-weaving history to follow paths long discarded by Western thought. 

I am striving to understand what this type of society would have done once (if) it grew to the point where it was an endemic species, meaning the very existence of the society is having a direct negative consequential effect on the bioregion or biosphere.  Once that society has recognized their impact what changes would they make or have made? This is a long term study and unlikely to ever come to an end. However, by using this lens to peer into an alternative rewoven historical past, I can infer some things to the Now of today's destructive, scarcity based society to try to pull from that vision as much as I can into reality today.  

Using this methodology, I have thus far come to these realizations that I am using in pursuing current projects: 

De-Commodification of land. In a regenerative society, one would not own the land any more than a sparrow or tree owns the land in today’s western societies. One would have responsibility to steward land and be in relationship with the land, but could not own the land, the land is “owned” by itself, meaning respecting the rights of a place in the societal / cultural context.

Realising the importance of Abundance. In a Regenerative society based on Abundance the concept of Enough is critical.  To that end I am looking at alternative economic models with special interest in wellness accounting, gift economies as well as groups that have a commitment to voluntary redistribution of wealth and a commitment to wholeness of the individual, group and planet as a foundation for the group's relationship with the land. 

"Everyone gets a nest, but not a nest egg”. In a regenerative society the concept of “home” would be vastly different from our western concept of home.  In this way of thinking, feeling, and doing, home is a place of refuge, security, care and relationship.  It would not resemble the ego-economic drivers that lead to our current glut of plastic mcmansion wealth creation devices that is the norm in Canada, United States, Australia and New Zealand.

De-Commodification of Intellectual Property.  In a regenerative society, intellectual property would be held in common. With common purpose, rapid innovation is possible.

As I have been gaining greater knowledge and understanding of larger issues and how our individual actions leave impact ripples on many different levels, I have tried to find an aligning principle around which I can harmonize all my actions personally, professionally, as a member of a family, a community, a province, a nation, a planet.  It seems that at the end of the day I keep coming back to some simple basics we all should know in our hearts.  Simply said, the concept is love --  Not sexual or fraternal love, but universal love, the concept Martin Lither King called the Beloved Community-- Recognizing the light in ourselves and all else.  For me, this is a fairly easy concept to understand  intellectually, but one that takes constant work and discipline to practice in the scrum of everyday life.  It is this goal of bringing light to all that I interact with, that has led to the aspirations represented in all my current work.

I know that this sounds very “out there”, but as a human and an architect. I have recognized that we must remove our egos from our professional vision and dedicate a newfound focus on achieving a balance between the individual and the collective to find our place as professionals within a larger socio economic system. Finding this balance is going to be hard, and very unsettling to many, but I believe it is imperative to finding our way through the current way of thinking to a new way of thinking, feeling, and doing.

career_highlights:
EHF Fellow Alex Fleming is leading transformative advancements in space exploration and aerospace technologies, leveraging over two decades of experience in the field. 
With a background in Aerospace Engineering from Georgia Tech and an MBA from Carnegie Mellon University, Alex's expertise spans vertical flight aircraft, renewable energy systems, space systems, mesh networks, interoperability and unmanned undersea vehicles.

Alex is the co-founder and CTO of iMETALX inc. , which provides in space servicing and space environment management solutions with a vision to create a future where space is accessible and sustainable for all. 
Under his leadership, iMETALX has secured key partnerships and grants, including with the US Air Force, US Space Force, US Navy, US Department of Energy, and Advanced Research Projects Agency-Energy (ARPA-e), and has developed breakthrough technologies in the areas of Space Domain Awareness, Resident Space Object Inspection, and Spacecraft Teaming.
Alex's technical leadership and strategic vision have enabled the development of cutting-edge technologies that are shaping the future of aerospace and renewable energy industries, positioning Alex as a leader in the field of sustainable technology innovation.
how_im_looking_to_support_the_nz_ecosyst: Fund Raising,  Ecosystem development
key_networks: Christchurch Aerospace
impact_goals_nz: Space Domain Awareness, In Space Servicing Assembly and Manufacturing (ISAM),  Ocean observation and deep sea exploration
how_to_support_my_work: Venture Capital
five_things_to_know:
Alex Fleming has demonstrated over 25 years of technological innovation leading diverse teams from conceptualization to designing, building and testing extreme-environment and high performance systems. He has made seminal contributions to Aerospace (vertical flight aircraft-V-22, XV-15, Joint Strike Fighter F-35 and military jet engines-ATEGG, F100 and F119), Renewable Energy Systems (>5MW Wind, Marine Hydro Kinetic-MHK and Energy Storage), Marine (unmanned undersea vehicles-US Navy,
data centers) and Autonomy ( drones, C2-mesh network communication, Beyond Line of Sight- BLOS, Sense and Avoid). He started his career at Georgia Tech Research Institute-GTRI under a freshman research recognition scholarship and NASA Ames working on Vertical Takeoff and Landing-VTOL aircraft pre cursors to air taxis today. At Pratt & Whitney he engineered various components of the Joint Strike Fighter F-35
VTOL Engine. He then joined Lockheed Martin in various engineering and management
positions over 7 years developing Autonomous Underwater Vehicles. He was CTO of Dehlsen Associates for 7 years where he led programs in Multi-MW ocean current, tidal, wave energy, energy storage, desal and underwater data centers. He was Principal Investigator on 8 grants developing the technology platforms, developed intellectual property, while raising TRL and TPL through capital de-risking using technoeconomic analysis. Led technical due diligence and fund
raising for strategic investment from fortune 50 and private equity companies towards
commercialization. Recently developed Beyond Line of Sight-BLOS drones working with
regulators, system engineering, communication, testing and flight demonstrations. Alex is presently co-founder and CTO of iMetalx Group. Alex Fleming is a graduate of Georgia Institute of Technology in Aerospace Engineering and received a Lockheed Martin Sponsored MBA from Carnegie Mellon, has multiple patents and resides in San Francisco.

five_things_to_know:
Alex Lee is a trained lawyer and filmmaker. He is the most senior Asian filmmaker and documentary storyteller in NZ and advocates strongly for better inclusion and representation for Asian filmmakers in NZ. Alex has taught film production in New Zealand and was previously Head of the Department of Performing & Screen Arts at UNITEC. 

Alex has been extensively involved as an advocate of as well as a capacity builder within the screen industry. He is the former Chair of Film Auckland Incorporated, a role he held this role since 2014.  

He is the Co-director and Founding Trustee of the Documentary New Zealand Trust (www.docedge.nz) that runs the Oscar qualifying Doc Edge International Film Festival, Doc Edge Forum, Doc Edge Pitch, Doc Edge Schools, Doc Edge Goldies, Doc Edge Exhibition and other documentary advocacy work. He co-curates the festival programme, designs the industry programme, selects guests to the events, chooses the Pitch finalists and interactive projects for the Exhibition. Alex has been a panellist and speaker on many international screen forums as well as a jury member of various leading film festivals overseas. He is also leading Good Pitch Aotearoa NZ  (www.goodpitchaotearoa.nz; www.docsociety.org) to be held in 2021. This is a world wide initiative across over 17 countries which has raised over USD $ 32 Million for impact campaigns for selected documentaries. The intention is to facilitate partnerships between filmmakers and subjects with partners that can help reach audiences and create impact.

Alex has advocated for greater support and visibility for the Asian screen and performing arts community. He was a Board Member of Creative Coalition, previously National Executive Member of the Screen Directors Guild of New Zealand, past Mayoral Think-tank on the creative industries as well as the China Club sub-committee of the NZ Film Commission.  He is a Board Member of Creative Coalition and a previous National Executive Member of the Screen Directors Guild of New Zealand. He was also on the past Mayoral Think-tank on the creative industries as well as the China Club sub-committee of the NZ Film Commission.

He brought to the screen the film Hip Hop-eration, a critically acclaimed feature documentary that won numerous awards internationally as well as 3 out of  4 documentary awards at the NZ Film Awards. He sourced the story-rights, crewed the team including the director and co-producer. He looked after the contracts, chain of title, festival, sales & distribution and was a key story creative. As a result of an agreement as to credits, he agreed to take the Executive Producer’s credit.
He also executive produced feature documentary Monterey, award winning short documentaries So Far Yet So Close, Makara at Makara and was a key mentor to director Yihwen Chen in her theatrical feature Eye on the Ball, currently in release in Malaysia. Alex has also supported numerous other feature documentaries include She Shears, Girl on the Bridge and Loimata.

He has a production company Yalla Media (www.yallamedia.co.nz) and is currently producing  two theatrical documentaries, Vrindavan - City of Widows (working title) – a NZ/Canadian coproduction and True Colours (a NZ Film Commission supported film) in development. Alex will also produce a cross platform theatrical documentary/installation The Return Home, a NZ/China coproduction in development. 
Alex was recently supported by the NZ Film Commission to attend a script development workshop of the fiction project Broken Journey which is inspired by The Return Home at EAVES – The Ties That Bind in Singapore. This was organised by Screen Singapore, Friuli Venezia Giulia Audiovisual Fund, EAVE, Udine Far East Film Festival and Southeast Asian Audio-Visual Association (SAAVA), and supported by the Creative Europe - MEDIA sub-programme of the European Union and Aurora Holdings).  

Alex is the Producer of a feature documentary in production called Shh…Diam, a NZ.Malaysia/Hong Kong coproduction. It has been awarded a Hot Docs Cross Current grant and has been pitched in various international pitch markets.

He is also the Producer of a short film Unnatural written by director Dan Giraud who has received a NZ Film Commission Hunga Taunaki ā Rorohko - Online Mentorship with Roger Donaldson. The film is currently in development and heading to pre-production.

A hybrid feature film known as True Colours is currently being developed with the support of the NZ Film Commission. Alex is attached as Producer.

Alex also directed an award winning short film Wong Cha Cha that played in international festivals.  Alex has also produced other award-winning long form and short form fiction including independent theatrical fiction feature The Last Magic Show.

career_highlights:
A primetime Emmy-winning producer, Alexandra currently co-heads the documentary division of TIME Studios.  Prior to TIME, she has worked most notably as a producer of feature documentaries for acclaimed directors Alex Gibney, Jehane Noujaim, Lauren Greenfield and Lucy Walker. 

From 2007-2012, Alexandra ran Alex Gibney’s company, Jigsaw Productions, managing all aspects of development and production, and paving the way for the companys's 2012 sale to Content Media Group and subsequent 2020 sale to Imagine Entertainment. 

Johnes’ documentaries have received several awards and nominations, including an Oscar nomination for Jehane Noujaim's THE SQUARE, the Grand Jury Prize and Audience Award at the Sundance Film Festival, a DGA award, several Peabody Awards, and several Primetime Emmys.

Other recent films include THE TERRITORY, HOLY HELL, GABY GIFFORDS WONT'S BACK DOWN, MEA MAXIMA CULPA: SILENCE IN THE HOUSE OF GOD, THE HOUSE I LIVE IN, RAFEA: SOLAR MAMA, REAGAN, MAGIC TRIP: KEN KESEY’S SEARCH FOR A KOOL PLACE, CLIENT 9: THE RISE AND FALL OF ELIOT SPITZER, CASINO JACK AND THE UNITED STATES OF MONEY, MY TRIP TO AL-QAEDA and FREAKONOMICS.

Production and distribution partners on her films have included Neon, Magnolia Pictures, Participant Media, Netflix Originals, Disney, A24, Universal, CNN Films, A&E, ITVS, BBC, ZDF/ARTE, Optimum Releasing, Film Rise, HBO and HBOMax.

Alexandra is an advisor and mentor to Film Independent and Sundance, a member of AMAPAS, the Academy of Motion Pictures of Arts & Sciences, the Television Academy, the PGA and the Guild of Future Architects.  She is a graduate of NYU's Tisch School of the Arts.

career_highlights:
Amy brings 20+ years of leadership experience designing and leading global initiatives that deliver social and environmental impact. She has worked across sectors, scales, and cultures building projects and partnerships and co-designing solutions to complex challenges. An expert in climate resilience, she has partnered with some of the world’s largest cities and smallest island nations to prepare for a changing future. 

As Principal of Weaver Collaborations, she partners with decision-makers in philanthropy and policy to localize their efforts and co-design more integrated solutions, weaving together rich expertise in nature, community, and climate.

She has helped stand up and lead two global philanthropic partnerships: 100 Resilient Cities at the Rockefeller Foundation and Resilient Reefs at the Great Barrier Reef Foundation. Both efforts partnered at local and national levels to transform policies and implement on-ground solutions for equitable climate action. She also helped lead and grow two urban research centers at New York University and worked in New York City government to advance community development.
impact_goals_nz:
I’m passionate about innovating solutions to adapt to climate change, improving governance in ways that rebalance power to local communities and disadvantaged and disenfranchised populations, and partnering with philanthropy to support all of that work in enduring ways. Aotearoa and the Pacific can be models for how to do that well. 

I seek to deepen connection and partnership between resilience practitioners and funders globally with those in Aotearoa New Zealand. And to build impactful partnerships and a pipeline of locally-owned, global best practice climate solutions across the blue continent.
five_things_to_know:
- I am a systems thinker and policy geek with a genuine love for organizational strategy and team building. 

- I’m passionate about innovating solutions to adapt to climate change and improving governance in ways that rebalance power to local communities and disadvantaged and disenfranchised populations. 

- I have a decade of experience working in philanthropy. I understand the sector’s desire to disrupt and scale all at once—the power, potential and pitfalls of those ideals. And I'm dedicated to helping make philanthropy more responsive and contextual. I would love to connect with folks also working in philanthropy.

- I’m a mom of a goofy and curious little one who is also figuring out how to do well for the world. We are learning a lot together. 

- I am a decent fly fisherwoman and an excellent bocce player.

impact_goals_nz: My first priority is to listen and learn from the wisdom of the local culture and feel into what is most true, in connection to the land and the original custodians.  My hope is that together we can model new ways of coordinating that shift behavior from win-lose to win-for-all outcomes. My experiments thus far tell me that blockchain and token-based currencies have a big role to play in reshaping how we govern, share value, fund projects, and increase our individual sovereignty while also improving the common good. Personal transformation, not just technology, will be required for such a coordination system to function; one that is based in love, increases unity, and unleashes the self-organizing intelligence that is.
how_to_support_my_work: Listen and love. Provide feedback on the Win-for-All framework. Participate in experiments.
five_things_to_know:
Since 2016, Andrew has been immersed in researching and testing a new framework that outlines the necessary and sufficient conditions to shift from a win-lose to win-for-all economy. The underlying premise, based on Game Theory, is that business behavior is primarily influenced by 6 game aspects: the objective, how choices are made, how people are developed, how people are motivated, how performance is assessed, and how benefits are distributed. The game of business changes when there is a change in these 6 aspects, which correlate to a shift in human development. Andrew is currently testing and implementing these ideas on organizations between 50 and 5,000 employees in India, Australia and the USA.
Watch Andrew's 20min presentation from the Planet Home Expo for a high level overview 
https://youtu.be/GlVx3tKHhiI

Pachamama Alliance
Board of Directors
www.Pachamama.org 
In alliance with indigenous wisdom, our programs focus on bringing forth an environmentally sustainable, spiritually fulfilling, socially just human presence on this planet. Our Awakening the Dreamer Symposium is in 100+ countries and impacted millions of lives. Our GameChangers Intensive and Drawdown Initiative collectively have 20,000+ participants. We’ve also initiated the permanent protection of more than 60 million acres of Amazon rainforest.  Andrew has been actively involved in the Pachamama Alliance since 2007 when he became a program facilitator and brought the Symposium to university campuses around North America. He was honored to join the Board of Directors in 2016 where he continues to serve.
https://youtu.be/dn-6U_SSaR8

GameChangers 500
Founder
www.GameChangers.co 
Andrew Hewitt is the Founder of GameChangers 500, an initiative that shines the spotlight on the world’s top For-Benefit businesses. Considered an alternative to lists like the Fortune 500, GameChangers 500 looks beyond revenue as the “point system” and awards organizations up to 11 badges that represent symbols of success in this “new game” of business. 
Trailer: https://youtu.be/WnCZ-Z9LZgg

GameChangers Unite
Founder
For over a decade, Andrew has been finding the GameChangers of our time and helping them unite through highly curated gatherings and transformational experiences. Over the years he’s improved his discernment in identifying the truly devoted from the merely ambitious. He has convened this growing network in various ways, including collaborations with the Fetzer Institute, OneProject, Hive, and the Pachamama Alliance. Through 2017 Andrew convened more than 100 of these GameChanging leaders to undergo a week-long transformational process that lead to profound healing, trust and coherence among participants. 


Fourth Sector Mapping Initiative
Advisory Council, Member of Various Working Groups
https://www.mapping.fourthsector.org/
There is a critical need to undertake a comprehensive research agenda to help policymakers better understand and address barriers facing for-benefit enterprises and determine strategies for promoting the fourth sector’s growth and impact. The Fourth Sector Mapping Initiative is a global, multi-stakeholder collaborative effort to develop a rigorous taxonomy of for-benefit organizations and a global census of these entities and their supportive ecosystems.
As one of the most active advisory council members and participants in various working groups, Andrew Hewitt has helped lead the process for classifying for-benefit organizations in service of various constituencies (policymakers, economic developers, job seekers, etc). He also moved to Encinitas California to spearhead a regional mapping initiative for modeling Fourth Sector economic development.

impact_goals_nz:
I believe that New Zealand has unique dynamics that make it an optimal place from which to build the new generation of holistic values-driven businesses that the world needs to thrive for the rest of this century.  I further believe that these businesses can scale globally from a base in New Zealand, rather than needing to relocate offshore.  I aim as an EHF Fellow to help make this happen.  

I also care deeply about the development of EHF as an organization, community, and force for global impact, and spend time representing and connecting EHF to other complementary global networks.
how_to_support_my_work: I'm always looking to meet the New Zealand based impact entrepreneurs with global ambition that I can be of greatest service to in realizing their vision and maximizing their impact.
five_things_to_know:
I'm an open source govtech entrepreneur, former government CIO, former space scientist, and angel investor passionate about contributing to ventures that build a more decentralized, egalitarian and democratic planet.

As an EHF Investor Fellow, I support New Zealand impact entrepreneurs working on govtech, spacetech, healthtech, and blockchain tech ventures.

My own core venture, CoverUS, aims to drive towards a more efficient and effective US health care system by putting patients in control of their own health data, and using it to get insights about how to better afford their care.  

I also manage ChainLink Capital Management, a capital management firm that invests in a range of digital asset and blockchain technology funds.  I support the maturation of decentralizing technologies because I believe they can help to restore our sovereignty over our personal information, and help to redefine and redistribute asset and transactional power in a more egalitarian manner.

career_highlights:
I am an economist, human rights expert, and entrepreneur with extensive experience across the non-profit, for-profit, and public sectors. My main entrepreneurial venture has been co-founding HRMI, a global non-profit producing the world's best and most comprehensive country-level human rights dataset. During my tenure as Executive Director of HRMI, I raised over $5m in philanthropic grants and donations, led the co-design of a range of trusted human rights data tools, and integrated collaborative and participatory leadership approaches into HRMI's organisational structure and governance. HRMI's global community includes more than 2000 human rights experts across the globe, and dozens of team members - including a combination of paid staff and volunteers.

My passion lies in driving systems change. I have degrees in Psychology and Economics from the University of Otago and a Masters of Public Affairs from Princeton University’s School of Public and International Affairs, which I attended on a Fulbright Scholarship. Before my transition into human rights, I worked as a senior economic adviser to governments.  

I continue to support HRMI to play a disruptive and positive leading role on the global human rights stage, and am exploring new avenues for impact, with a particular interest in ethical wealth management.

I have significant experience in the for-profit sector, including founding a company, and being part of network of CEOs dedicated to building global enterprises from New Zealand.
how_im_looking_to_support_the_nz_ecosyst: I am keen to support EHF to achieve its innovation potential.

Princeton Alumni Network
impact_goals_nz:
"Basecamp for a better world" really resonates with me. I believe that Aotearoa can play a huge role in this, but it's up to us to make it happen.  

"The best way to predict the future is to build it." (one of my favourite quotes)
how_to_support_my_work: The non-profit I co-founded (HRMI) is always looking for new collaborators: please get in touch if you would like us to expand our human rights data coverage in your country or if you can connect us with people at organisations who may be interested in using our data in their work. We are currently funded entirely by grants and donations so connections to potential funders or donors would be amazing.  Thank you!
five_things_to_know:
I'm a kiwi entrepreneur, and mum to a gorgeous teenage boy. I'm one of the founders of the Human Rights Measurement Initiative (HRMI) - (pronounced "her-mee"). Our goal is to disrupt the status quo and get more global leaders and decision-makers focused on improving people's lives. 

We established credibility for producing accurate and robust data, accepted by both human rights practitioners and measurement experts, and we are working to realise our vision of a world where countries are competing to see who can treat people the best.

Check out our country scores at rightstracker.org.  

For a brief period (mainly over 2024) I led a company - Rights Intelligence - which aimed to get new human rights based products and data tools into mainstream financial institutions. Demand was slower than anticipated so the company is now in  hibernation and the dataset we developed is instead available directly from HRMI.

Other things in life that I love include live music, dancing, good food, spending time with friends and family, and meditation.
anything_else_to_share: I am now on sabbatical exploring next steps. I have a hunch my next career will be something to do with innovation in the area of wealth management. I'd love to discuss this with anyone interested!

Recent releases include “Fools & Dreamers: Regenerating a Native Forest”, "Together We Grow", and “Living the Change: Inspiring Stories for a Sustainable Future”.
how_im_looking_to_support_the_nz_ecosyst: By sharing through film the stories of people and projects showcasing ways to live more lightly on earth. In this way we're able to support that work and help spread its message and inspiration. Nearly all of our films are free to view online, in order to create as few barriers as possible to sharing these high-impact stories.
impact_goals_nz:
Since 2016, we have steadily grown both our audience and our reputation for making high-quality, solutions-focused films that inspire people to ‘live the change’. We know from our own experience, however, that it can be hard to turn enthusiasm into action in a world that is, paradoxically, under-resourced for living lightly and creating a fair share for all. One of the questions driving us right now is, What resources can we contribute along with our films that will ensure our audience members become not just enthusiasts but activists for a healthier, more resilient planet and people? Our intention is to generate or collaborate on resources as part of the release of our films that help our audience to reduce the barriers to living lightly on planet earth.

In addition, we’ve seen the need to broaden our reach so that our films impact and resonate with people beyond our white, middleclass, able-bodied, privileged perspective. Here in Aotearoa New Zealand we live on lands dishonestly colonised, within a culture that is fundamentally racist. Another question driving us is, How can we ensure our films contribute to healing both our culture and our planet? First we must properly educate ourselves, exploring how we can be good and productive Treaty of Waitangi/Te Tiriti o Waitangi partners, and ensuring diversity exists in our film content and in the people we work with to make our films happen.
how_to_support_my_work:
- By sharing our films widely.
- By letting us know about potential stories to tell.
- By helping us to form connections within the regeneration/sustainability/community resilience/social justice etc. spaces.
- With funding, from a small one-off donation to financing a whole film via our profile on Open Collective. 

Information about how we operate can be found on our website.
five_things_to_know:
- I'm interested in telling stories that help to guide us in healing planet and people.
- Because I sometimes feel despair for the state of the world, my work and life are all about finding the joy and hope.
- I'm an introvert in denial about the fact she thrives most when she gets out and socialises more :)
- I spent 20 years in the book publishing industry before transitioning to film.
- I lived in Buenos Aires for six years just so I could spend my nights dancing tango.

career_highlights: My professional career, over 35 years, has been focused on the intersection of energy and the environment and with a particular focus on renewable energy.  My experiences include being an entrepreneur, developer, investor and consultant; and my last position was building and running a renewable energy investment group for a major bank, where over the course of 14 years we became one of the largest investors in wind and solar projects in the U.S.  For many years I have also focused efforts on clean energy access and particularly through my work with the non-profit We Care Solar and their STEM education group, We Share Solar.  Most recently, I have teamed up with other EHF Fellows in supporting and advising Kia Kotahi Ako Trust, including work on the development of the We Share Solar STEM module for students, teachers and communities in New Zealand.

impact_goals_nz: After a highly successful pilot in 2021 of We Share Solar at Haeata Community Campus in Christchurch, we have launched additional pilots and are expanding into other schools throughout the country.  This program is part of the broader work of Kia Kotahi Ako Trust, which is focused on incubating and activating education initiatives (such as We Share Solar) in order to create systems-level change across climate, wellbeing and health, through learning and education.  To create this change, Kia Kotahi is focused on key impact areas of (1) climate and science education and (2) equitable models of learning and wellbeing.
how_to_support_my_work: Kia Kotahi is seeking support of its work in a variety of ways, including financial support, connections and advising.

Also, I am an Advisor to Kia Kotahi Ako Trust, a New Zealand charity founded by EHF Fellow Joanne McEachen and whose CEO is EHF Fellow Sarah Grant.  Several other Fellows are involved in various capacities of Kia Kotahi, as well, and the We Share Solar STEM module is one of the components of the charity.

career_highlights: A molecular biologist from Uganda, Blanche has been researching antibiotic-resistant bacterial strains. In New Zealand, she is developing a wearable frontline medical device that will detect and track antibiotic-resistant bacterial strains in real-time.
how_im_looking_to_support_the_nz_ecosyst: I will share my technology for integration with the NZ health and agricultural sectors, bringing effective antibiotic resistance real-time tracking solutions to their farms and health institutions. Msawo will also provide employment opportunities to New Zealanders.

impact_goals_nz:
New Zealand’s rate of antibiotic resistance is comparatively low, but it is getting worse due to increasing antibiotic use and some types of infections becoming more resistant. I am working on a plan that will help reduce and maintain the country’s antibiotic resistance levels, low.

Goal 1: To improve the quality and timeliness of antibiotic prescribing, reduce the unnecessary use of antibiotics, and prevent the spread of resistant infections. 

Goal 2: To enhance the surveillance, detection, and investigation of resistant outbreaks, link illnesses to sources, such as food or animal contact, and inform public health interventions.
how_to_support_my_work:
Creating a medical device is a complex process that involves various aspects, including regulatory compliance, technical development, testing, and market considerations. Here are some ways my work may be supported (using connections, funding, and research collaborations) throughout the different stages of medical device development.

1.	Regulatory Guidance:  Work with regulatory experts or consultants who have experience in the medical device industry. They can help me navigate complex regulatory requirements and ensure the device complies with local and international standards.

2.	Research and Development:  Collaborate with experienced engineers and scientists in the field to develop and refine the device. Universities, research institutions, and private R&D companies can provide valuable insights and expertise.

3.	Clinical Expertise:  Engage with healthcare professionals, clinicians, and key opinion leaders in the relevant medical field. Their input can help refine the device to meet actual clinical needs and improve its usability.

4.	Prototyping and Manufacturing:  Work with experienced product design and manufacturing firms to create prototypes and finalize the manufacturing process. These organizations can help ensure that the device is scalable, reliable, and cost-effective.

5.	Funding and Investment:  Seek funding from investors, venture capitalists, or government grants to support the development and commercialization of the medical device. Financial support is crucial for research, development, and regulatory compliance.

6.	Collaborate with Incubators and Accelerators: Join medical device-focused incubators or accelerators. These programs often provide mentorship, resources, and networking opportunities to help startups navigate the challenges of bringing a medical device to market.

7.	Clinical Trials:  If applicable, plan and conduct clinical trials with the guidance of clinical research experts. This step is essential for demonstrating the safety and efficacy of the medical device, and it often requires collaboration with medical institutions.

8.	Intellectual Property Protection:  Consult with legal experts specializing in intellectual property to ensure proper protection of the inventions through patents, trademarks, or other relevant mechanisms.

9.	Quality Management Systems (QMS):  Implement a robust QMS to ensure that the device meets quality standards. ISO certifications, such as ISO 13485 for medical devices, are often essential for regulatory compliance.

10.	Market Research and Commercialization:  I will conduct thorough market research to understand my target audience, competition, and potential market challenges. Develop a strong commercialization strategy, including marketing and distribution plans.

11.	Networking and Industry Associations:  Attend industry conferences, workshops, and networking events to connect with professionals in the medical device field. Joining industry associations can provide valuable resources and networking opportunities.

Seeking support from experts in each relevant area is crucial for the success of the medical device. Building a multidisciplinary team and establishing strategic partnerships will significantly enhance my chances of successfully navigating the complexities of medical device development.

We also rely on EHF fellows to convey our work and its significance to the world, with the increasingly prevalent threat of antibiotic resistance on a global scale.

five_things_to_know:
Originally from Winnipeg - I’ve spent the last 8 years going back and forth from Toronto and San Francisco. 

My entrepreneurial adventure started in University, after joining my best friend's venture. Deco, was a drive up, on-site windshield repair service that we scaled from 3 to 100+ locations across Central and Eastern Canada, and 130 employees within 4 years.

While scaling this business (and dropping out of University) I was drawn to the newly launched iPad, and developed an internal iOS platform that helped us scale DECO so rapidly. The success of this mobile platform is what lead to the creation of Joist.

Joist, is the largest contractor network in the world. It helps contractors estimate, invoice, process payments, and mange their business directly in the field. Joist has helped over 1M contractors, and helps facilitate $30B+ in estimates & invoices respectively. In 2018, we sold Joist to EverCommerce a firm backed by Silver Lake and Providence Equity Partners.

There were many lessons learned with Deco and Joist. I once had to lay off a significant portion of my team at Joist, which was embarrassing and felt like failure. After 3 years, I had re-built the team, drove the company to profitability, and received an acquisition offer which we took in December of 2018.

I have a passion for riding motorcycles, CrossFit, studying geo-politics, meditating, live music shows, and experimenting with my health.

Favourite books include: What Makes Sammy Run?, Enders Game, Open, and The Hard Thing about Hard Things.

I’m currently taking a "break" before jumping into my next venture! Focused on advising startups, and making 5-10 early stage investments per year.

Beyond just investments, with the help of my company, I am operating an investment ecosystem, called “Impact Collective,” a passionate community of entrepreneurs, investors, and industry experts, in which they gather, discuss and evaluate projects, and mentor and advocate their favorite projects. I envision introducing ‘collective intelligence’ to impact investing mechanism. My blog explains in more detail about why I am committed to the community approach: https://bit.ly/2X7gJpS
five_things_to_know:
In 2008, I co-founded Viki.com, a community-powered video translation platform, introducing Asian video content to the people in the Western world. With tens of thousands of voluntary translators collaborating to produce subtitles for international TV shows in more than 130 languages, Viki’s community has produced 100M lines of subtitle text while acquiring +40M users from around the world. In 2013, Viki was acquired by Rakuten for $200M. 

After the exit of Viki, I moved to Korea and started TheVentures, an accelerator and early-stage investment company, in 2014. From the days of Viki, the keywords driving my vision have always been “community” and “cross-border.”  In addition to investing in Korea-based startups, my accelerator also started to invest in the teams based in or expanding to South East Asia, India, and the US. My firm has backed +80 teams and made 13 successful M&As and exits while helping 70% of our portfolio companies receive follow-on investments.

My current initiative is “impact investing” focusing on opportunities in APAC. My accelerator is running Impact Collective Program, designed to discover, incubate, and invest in impact-driven startups based in APAC or targeting the APAC market.  Community is the key component in both evaluation process and incubation process of the program. We aim to redefine the capital model for startups by bringing the collective power of community and local experts into the investment and incubation process.

Co-founder of Generation Zero, helped get Action Station, a non-profit community campaigning organisation, off the ground, and joined Enspiral and the Loomio teams a few months after they incorporated. impact_goals_nz:
Big picture, I want to help New Zealand be an example for the western world in policy, business, culture, environment and more. This work started in 2009 for me when myself and friends began the work of improving New Zealand climate policy, which resulted in the creation of Generation Zero (generationzero.org) and the acceptance 10 years later of the Zero Carbon Bill into government.  Aotearoa New Zealand is a mixture of such incredible things. As a kiwi I feel it in my bones, I see the highest potential of the bicultural, tech innovation, punching above our weight yet grounding humility combination. I also see the ways in which NZ plays small, and as someone who has lived around the world I aim to hold a mirror to other kiwis to also remind everyone they're world leaders. 

Stagnation, nationalism, and stress internationally in 2020 is yet more reason to create a bastion of hope and showcasing an alternative. Monetary policy, climate policy, agricultural policy, city planning policy, space policy, the resource management act and more can all be sources if immense innovation for NZ to shine and create local wellbeing as well as global inspiration. Policy entrepreneurship is as systemic aspect of change making, and can be done through many routes. Nonprofits and for profits alike have roles to play alongside and with the gov't and policy makers themselves - let's connect if this is also your world view. 

Changing the rules of the game feels more sensible at this point than playing the existing one. No single issue is the issue. I am interested in modifying our social, governance operating systems for the better, such that many verticals benefit.
how_to_support_my_work: Help to weave and enhance the narrative that Space and Earth are a shared policy domain which needs new governance processes for effective management of Earth resources and space resources for the sustainability of life.
five_things_to_know: Chelsea has a background in complex problem solving with a systems change approach. She has worked on climate policy, suicide reduction, democratic process, commons management, open source technology more. With a firm belief that the “how” is as important as the “what”, a lot of her work centers on the ways we work together, talk, listen and connect. She has been leading the Open Lunar Foundation as the COO since early 2018. Open Lunar seeks to catalyse a peaceful, cooperative lunar settlement on the Moon driven by the best human values as a truly international effort.

search_tags: Regenerative Agriculture, Sustainability, Environment, Conservation, AI for good, Oct22
ventures: AgServer, The Agriculture is Sexy (a.k.a Agricrocks) Network
career_highlights: Built Green Stalks & Foliage, an innovative aquaculture business from the ground up. Founder of the Agriculture-is-Sexy Network, which uses unconventional approaches to encourage youth to consider agriculture as a career path and serves 10,000 active community members from 100 countries. Recently appointed to Cargill’s Advisory Council.
how_im_looking_to_support_the_nz_ecosyst:
I look forward to supporting the start-up ecosystem in New Zealand with special  interest in sharing my knowledge with Māori youth on lean start-up mechanisms, especially with the view of kick-starting ventures with very meagre resources.

I also want to seek out ways of supporting other Fellows working on regenerative agriculture, renewables, climate action and reduction of waste to engender sustainable production and consumption.

Generally I am very disposed to helping anyone who wishes to understand more about Africa’s changing dynamics as home to some of the world’s fastest growing and emerging economies, especially in the event that such also offers a full cycle benefit for New Zealand.
key_networks:
Global Farmer Network 

Nigeria Economic Summit Group (NESG)

Nigeria Leadership Initiative (NLI)

Tony Elumelu Entrepreneurship Pan –African Network 

US Consulate Lagos, Carrington Fellowship

Alliance for Science Global Leadership Fellowship at Cornell University

Youth Alliance for Leadership and Development in Africa (YALDA)
impact_goals_nz:
I want to help connect New Zealand’s Agribusinesses to the African Agrifood ecosystem in such a way that a profitable, symbiotic and economic viable relationship is created for both parties. Through this sort of partnership, New Zealand can export its innovation, technical competencies, technologies, machineries and proven processes  to Africa which can then help bolster food production on the continent. 

New Zealand already estimated that it can feed 40 million people and wants to double its agricultural exports by 2025. This kind of arrangement will help in not only meeting but surpassing these targets. It will also lead to preserving New Zealand's limited pristine environment as the bulk of the production will be done in Africa which holds more than 60 percent of the world’s uncultivated arable land.
how_to_support_my_work:
I am looking for New Zealand agri-companies with offerings that can be extended to Africa. This includes companies in already one or two African countries but want to expand to other African countries, such as Nigeria, which is the largest market on the continent. Access to other stakeholder institutions such as the Callaghan Innovation is also very critical.

Some of the solutions that I am working on require in-depth knowledge of Machine Learning, AI, open source, distributed governance, and organising decentralised systems. I am very open to such guidance.
five_things_to_know:
Chibuike Emmanuel is a farmer, development entrepreneur and environmentalist who is passionate about bringing farmers views to the fore because they are rarely invited to the table when decisions are being made about them. He leads Green Stalks & Foliage, an innovative agro-allied outfit and is the founding curator of the Agriculture is Sexy Network which mainstreams young people into agri-food careers, using unconventional approaches as the average age of farmers continues to dwindle.

Recently he started developing Agserver, a free peer-to-peer knowledge transfer digital platform for especially smallholder farmers to crowd-source information by  asking questions and getting answers from one another, without leaving their farms and by making use of simple mobile phones with or without the internet.

Chibuike currently serves as a Technical Assistant to the Nigerian Government in drafting the National Development Plan for the next 30 years (2050) as part of the Thematic Working Group on Agriculture, Food Security and Rural Development. He is also a Bridge Research Fellow at the Nigeria Economic Summit Group (NESG), Nigeria’s foremost private sector led think tank, where he contributes to the Agriculture and Food Security Policy Commission. Furthermore he is a member of the Cargill Animal Nutrition Advisory Council.

Living in the Niger Delta region of Nigeria which battles with gross environmental degradation and understanding the intersection between agriculture and the environment, he cofounded EDEN GCP - an environmental sustainability initiative that has reached about half a million people with its sensitization efforts and is now working on launching Nigeria’s first eco shop. He has been privileged to share his thoughts widely at fora such as the World Food Prize Borlaug Dialogue, the World Bank Headquarters and with the staff of the US Senate Committee on Ag.

I am a global technology executive and serial co-founder of initiatives that use technology for the public good.   I have built, invested in, and mentored companies from startup through multiple investment rounds and exit.  I have led teams that built some of the largest implementations of low-connectivity mobile phone applications in the world, the first public-sector drone/UAV corridors, and various financial vehicles that have helped bring together the best of public sector values and technological innovation.

I co-founded UNICEF's Innovation Unit, and built a world-class team of open-source technologists that propelled UNICEF to the forefront of innovation for public good.  I led the teams that set up UNICEF's Venture Fund, as well as the organization's Cryptocurrency Fund.  These are both the first vehicles of their kind in the UN.   I have advised two UN Secretary-Generals on new technologies, and worked with Heads of State and CEOs to set responsible, forward-looking technology policies.  My portfolio of interests and leadership experience spans near-space, blockchain, data science, drones and UAVs, and augmented reality applications.  Now, with Giga, I am focused on structured financing to deliver affordable connectivity to every school in the world.

five_things_to_know:
What Would It Mean to You to Use Neurotechnology to Read and Learn Three Times as Much in the Next Year?
What would you want to learn?  How would it impact your goals.  Looking forward to hearing.

Your Life Dream
Who are you in your greatest dream? How will you feel? Where will you be?  What will you have achieved? Who will be with you?
 
Your Leadership
Leaders were often born into personal adversity, choosing to take on the world's greatest challenges. The kettle boils most fiercely over the hottest fire. The greater the leader, the greater the crowd or mountain that they must move. From a lifetime of carrying the weight and facing challenge, leaders develop immense strength, resilience, courage, character. Think of the life journey of Nelson Mandela, Albert Einstein, Mother Teresa, Mohandas Ghandi, and where it brought them in their own life journey and in history.
 
Your Neural Functioning
Our nervous system is an incomplete evolutionary work-in-progress. The brain is replete with unnecessary bugs, creating your personal hardships, weaknesses and limitations. These slow down your journey and keep most of us from ever truly reaching our dreams.

The neurophysiology you were born with has created your mind, your emotions, your body, the voice in your mind and heart, your beliefs, your courage. Recent advances in neurotechnology now allow us to master, shape and overcome our inborn and learned constraints.
 
Investments in Yourself
Think about your physical body, the time and effort you invest every year supporting it: training, exercising, diet, sleep, appearance, health, wellness. What is your weekly investment in your neurophysiological capacity and resiliency?

How will the path to your life dream feel different with your internal efficacy and resilience elevated, allowing you to take flight, with greater serenity during determination, and higher connection with greater ease?

What investments do you make to train and optimize your nervous system, the wellspring of your actions, your intentions, your courage, and your connection with others, compared with your physical body?  What, exactly, would you be prepared to do to live in greater joy, with more time, less distraction, greater force and character, greater focus to bring to your life journey? 
 
Neurotechnology for Self-Mastery
Neurotechnology can provide mastery of yourself.  It can provide training that is increasingly powerful, enjoyable, necessary, and accessible to you. You will not need wires, drugs, nor invasive procedures, anymore than you do for training your physical body. Those around you, the world's leaders, will be racing ahead using this technology. 
 
Neurotechnology for Everyone
Together, all humans can now increasingly overcome the limitations that each of us started out in life with. We can optimize our own neural functioning for peak performance, just like we work to optimize our physical body functioning.
This vision is the core of my life's work in neuroscience research, neurotechnology development, entrepreneurship, and investing.

how_im_looking_to_support_the_nz_ecosyst:
I can contribute through mentoring, connecting with key resources, investor and change maker networks, and hard and soft skills. I have deep experience in branding and design, including UX development and creating the face of companies and clearly communicating complex ideas. five_things_to_know:
Since the early 2000's Conor worked in film and startups, shooting the first High Definition feature documentary in an active war zone, developing commercial projects for the likes of Coca-Cola and BMW Designworks, helping a startup grow to 40 people (before going to zero in the financial crisis), and looking for things in the ground. After 2008, Conor moved to Hong Kong to work for one of the world's leading independent macro investment research firms, focusing on long term implications of technology and alternative energy. Conor has since published over 30 papers, presented at conferences around the world, and helped start a number of companies from ad based donations to drinkable super food meals. Co-founding ChainofThings.com in 2016, as one of the first companies focused on blockchain’s potential to support the Internet of Things, he is currently focused on leveraging distributed technologies for positive environmental, humanitarian, and industrial applications. 

Most recently, Conor has helped to build Blockpass.org, a joint venture under Chain of Things that is focused on self-sovereign zero knowledge proof identity for the internet of everything. With Blockpass off and running with over 30 people on the international team, Conor is now focused on the development of Liquidstar.io - rentable off-grid smart batteries that run on Blockpass identity rails, targeting the 1.1 billion people who do not have access to electricity - SDG7. He also helped to found and develop LabofLabs.io, a coordinated initiative across the world’s top academic research labs to build an interoperable Knowledge ecosystem between academia and industry to aggressively SOLVE the UN Global Goals by 2030. Also in development is the secret project www.xcorp.io (Exploration Corporation) as the drive to curve advanced technology deployment for light touch exploration of land, sea, air, and space.

Also has a crush on space elevators, helium 3, photonic propulsion, and solar lensing ;)

ventures: I Am The River, The River Is Me, Myanmar Diaries, ZINDOC

career_highlights:
Corinne van Egeraat is an independent filmmaker and producer based in the Netherlands.

She is the winner of the 2022 Oscar-qualifying Berlinale Documentary Award for her most recent hybrid documentary Myanmar Diaries (2022). She has spent over ten years working on Myanmar as well as teaching human rights film there.

Her award-winning creative documentary films have been broadcast in over thirty countries and screened at more than four hundred and fifty film festivals around the world, premiering at the Berlinale, Venice, IDFA, and Rotterdam. 

In 2024, she released I AM THE RIVER, THE RIVER IS ME, a feature documentary about Aotearoa NZ's Whanganui River, the first river in the world to be recognised as a legal person. 

IN 2025, she will soon release The Coriolis Effect, a poetic creative documentary that also invites us to re-think our relationship to the natural world. It is a documentary about our world spinning out of control, set in Cape Verde, known as the place where hurricanes are born.
how_im_looking_to_support_the_nz_ecosyst: I'd like to offer my assistance in any way I can to help NZ and Oceania documentary filmmakers. I regularly teach and mentor personal/documentary storytelling, and like creating custom-made collaborative projects and workshops - with local filmmakers and artists in the countries where we are based.
impact_goals_nz:
As a documentary filmmaker and creative producer, the main topic in Aotearoa NZ I wish to pursue is how to advance the rights of nature.

Last year, we released our feature documentary about the Whanganui River, I AM THE RIVER, THE RIVER IS ME.  The film has been released internationally and in Aotearoa NZ. Together with the Whanganui Iwi, we are developing an impact campaign with the film.
how_to_support_my_work:
I am interested in partnerships with those who connect to my field and themes, in the broadest sense: to inspire each other, explore, co-create and collaborate.

With our documentaries, I regularly set up impact and outreach activities, ranging from big free open air screenings to smaller scale impact events and workshops. I am also specifically interested to connect to impact partners for these activities with WE ARE THE RIVER.
five_things_to_know:
I am a Dutch filmmaker and producer, based in Amsterdam, specialised in creative documentaries on injustice and climate change. I work together with my husband, cinematographer Petr Lom, who is also an EHF fellow. 

We make international films, screening in cinemas, A-film festivals worldwide and television. We base ourselves long-term in the places where we film, and have lived together in Egypt, where we made two films (Back to the Square and Ana Ana), in Myanmar (Burma Storybook and Myanmar Diaries), in the US (Angels on Diamond Street), Cape Verde (The Coriolis Effect - in production), and now in NZ where we have just completed filming WE ARE THE RIVER, a film about NZ's Whanganui River, the first river in the world recognized by law as a legal person and spiritual being.  

More about these films: 

Myanmar Diaries (2022).  A hybrid feature documentary about life under the regime of terror in Myanmar in the aftermath of its military coup of February 1st 2021, told through personal stories by a group of anonymous young Burmese filmmakers. Winner of the Oscar-qualifying Berlinale Documentary Award and 14 other international awards to date as it continues its international roll out. 

Burma Storybook is a creative documentary about the political transition in Myanmar, seen through the eyes of dissident Burmese poets. With the film, we have also published a Burma Storybook - poetry and photography book together with one of the Netherland's most renowned photographers Dana Lixenberg. Release 2016.

Angels on Diamond Street is a creative documentary film about three inspiring women fighting for social justice in an African American church in North Philadelphia. Their compassion invites us to forget about borders, and inspires all to go out and do something. Release 2019.

The Coriolis Effect is currently in production: a creative documentary about our world spinning out of control, with stories about climate change and how we are at risk of losing our global home; based in Cape Verde (also know as 'the place where hurricanes are born'). Release 2024.

We are the River is about to begin post-production: a creative documentary about NZ's Whanganui River, the first river in the world recognized by law as a legal person and spiritual being. Release 2024.

impact_goals_nz:
I believe a more vibrant, entrepreneurial, and innovative economy is a key driver of economic, social, environmental, and personal success in Aotearoa NZ.

I'm working towards that vision by creating and supporting initiatives which work towards 8 key ecosystem development goals (EDGs) outlined in my recent paper Start NZ Up: An Action plan For An More Innovative And Entrepreneurial Economy in Aotearoa New Zealand.

- EDG 01: NZ’s startup ecosystems need to be geographically concentrated and nationally connected
- EDG 02: NZ needs a coordinated national entrepreneurship plan
- EDG 03: NZ’s startups need capital to grow
- EDG 04: NZ’s startups need access to world-class expertise
- EDG 05: NZ startups need tech-savvy talent
- EDG 06: NZ needs more entrepreneurs
- EDG 07: NZ needs to reduce the cost of doing business with the rest of the world
- EDG 08: NZ needs corporate participation in the startup ecosystem

Find out more about this work at startnzup.com
how_to_support_my_work:
I believe that entrepreneurship is one of the most important ways to have personal impact in the world, and whilst they say it ‘takes a village to raise a startup’, the value created by all participants of that village are rarely distributed back in fair proportions to help the entire village grow. 

Navigating that journey is unnecessarily hard, often exclusive, and primarily rewards founders and financiers, not everyone else who helps create value along the journey.

Positive Sum DAO (+Σ DAO) aims to solve that problem by creating a peer-to-peer community of startups, founders, and startup community ‘enablers’ who collaborate to help entrepreneurs and startups grow, using open networks, shared resources, and in a way that grows a positive-sum community whereby all value created is captured, and distributed back to participating members in a more open & equitable way using Web3 as a platform.

I'm all about rolling your sleeves up and getting stuck in - and that's the best way you can help - by sharing your knowledge, networks, and know-how with the next generation of people who want to change the world.

Please do reach out if you want to help get involved in building this future - I'd love to talk!
five_things_to_know:
Kia ora, I'm Dan.

I'm passionate about building a stronger entrepreneurial economy in and from New Zealand.  

Given the right education, the right tools, and the right opportunities, I believe that everyone has the ability to create the future, instead of being constrained by the rules and work that others want them to do.

After an early career building high growth, venture-capital fuelled global startup companies from idea through to scale and eventual exit, I've spent the last 15 years giving back to New Zealand by leading the national startup community and creating key pieces of missing entrepreneurial support infrastructure here, allowing me to personally inspire, educate, and unlock the entrepreneurial potential of over 4,000 future Kiwi leaders.

I'm currently at the early stages of a new venture idea democratising access to networks, mentorship, and support for other entrepreneurs.

career_highlights:
Dr. Daniel Cordaro is the Founder and CEO of the Contentment Foundation. He is an Ashoka Fellow, Edmund Hillary Fellow, UBS Global Visionary, and faculty member at Columbia University. 

Dr. Cordaro led three of the largest cross-cultural studies on human emotion ever conducted in the field of psychology, which are featured in the textbook Understanding Emotions. He has spent the last decade of his career studying what it means to live a flourishing life and sharing the findings with schools around the world. His research teams have studied over a dozen cultures across the globe, including making first contact with an isolated community in remote Eastern Bhutan.

Dr. Cordaro earned his Ph.D. in Psychology and M.S. in Organic Chemistry from UC Berkeley, and later spent two years as the Director of Wellbeing at the Yale Center for Emotional Intelligence. While there, he and his team inaugurated a new area of research on contentment by publishing some of the first original research on what it means to cultivate unconditional acceptance of the present moment.

Above all, Dr. Cordaro is a lover of truth, wisdom, and indestructible wellbeing. Along his journeys around the world, he became a dedicated meditator, introspector, and questioner of assumptions - as his studies in philosophy began to unlock the mysteries of human flourishing. The culmination of this work now expresses through the Contentment Foundation as a service to humanity. Daniel’s personal audacious goal is to significantly improve the lives of one billion children and one billion adults - 25% of earth’s population - within his lifetime.
key_networks: Ashoka Fellowship, UBS Global Visionary Fellowship, Columbia University
impact_goals_nz: We intend to bring Tier 1 Mental Health services to every school across New Zealand in five years time.

name: Danika Revell
bio_tagline: We're eliminating period inequity in Aotearoa by 2030 through Conversation, Education + Access.
currently_based_in: Auckland, Auckland 1010 New Zealand
search_tags: period, periods, period poverty, period inequity, period equity, mensuration, menstrual, winter22
key_links: Danika Revell LinkedIn, Danika Revell Twitter, Email, Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, Twitter, Website
career_highlights: Co-founder and CEO of The Period Place with a vision for period equity by 2030, working with period-industry leading players, local and national government officials, researchers, product makers, innovators, social enterprises, and individuals.
how_im_looking_to_support_the_nz_ecosyst:
I am in both mentor and mentee relationships at the moment, and I thrive from both.

As we grow, we are looking for more people with strategic social and community impact experience to help us grow The Period Place all around Aotearoa.

We are also opening up relationships with the period industry in Australia (product providers, researchers and NFPs) to create a trans-Tasman period equity bubble.

We like to think of ourselves as a big cog in the centre moving lots of other cogs, from global period product players, to Kiwi big box retailers, Government Ministries, through to community groups and individuals.
key_networks:
The Period Place Leadership Committee
Pacific Women's Watch
Auckland Women's Health Council
impact_goals_nz:
Our goal is to eliminate period inequity from Aotearoa by 2030, in line with the UN SDGs.

We have created the Period Equity Ladder of Aotearoa - it spreads the 1.2 million menstruators across 4 rungs, and provides the framework for eliminating period inequity by 2030.

For the next two years (2021 and 2022) we will be focused on the bottom of the ladder - lifting the 70,000 people experiencing absolute period poverty month on month out of that, up to at least rung 3. 

As we are achieving this goal over the next 2 years, we will then open up our next goal of moving all people from rung 3 up to rung 2, and set our next timelines. 

We have a new principal partner (The Warehouse) helping us strategically set up a national framework of donation sites (their 92 stores), and are partnering with existing Community Groups (our Impact Partners), providing them with mentoring and education so that they can provide the products to those in need in their communities. 

We have new Major Product Partners who are supporting The Period Place with donations and donated period products (we will have provided more than 55,000 disposable products to individuals in absolute period poverty come November over a 6 month period), and have other Product Partners coming on board to support with whatever they can (from one-wāhine teams who sew their own reuseable pads). 

We will be co-designing an Impact Platform with the period industry to share results of our collective efforts, in order to avoid duplication of mahi and to share our data so we know the point at which period equity in Aotearoa has been achieved.
how_to_support_my_work:
We are seeking financial investment and people power investment as we rapidly grow, and are keen to explore any out of the box thinking that people bring to us in terms of flipping the way charities operate.

Personally, I am looking for more mentee opportunities with people who have gone through the young-kids-growing-organisation phase.

I have a 1 year old and 3 year old, and life outside The Period Place is almost a faster pace! Having a mentor who has been through it (any gender identity) would be beneficial to me personally, but also to The Period Place as a whole.
five_things_to_know:
Cofounder and CEO of The Period Place | Social Entrepreneur | Speaker

I am a born communicator and ideas woman. Cofounding The Period Place brought together the culmination of all my learnings in earlier careers across sectors and industries to bring about change for individuals, communities and the country. 

The Period Place works at the critical intersection of menstruators and their needs by creating sustainable change for them, their families, and their communities through our three mechanisms for change: Conversation, Education + Access.

In Aotearoa, and as a global community, it is our responsibility to invest in research to understand the links between menstruation and social and physical health outcomes so that we can change the systems that reinforce period inequity and make lasting changes for people with periods.

We want Aotearoa to be the first country in the world to achieve period equity by
2030, in line with the Sustainable Development Goals from the UN, and we're on track to achieve that goal with support from our Strategic Partners, and Impact Partners out in communities.

impact_goals_nz:
Investing in the next generation of impact focused early stage startups in New Zealand, Australia and beyond. 

I am involved in and want to be a part of more high impact ventures from Aotearoa that help move New Zealand forward.  

I have a particular interest in restoring and regenerating native forests and cleaning our waterways as well as alternative, novel and sustainable uses of land and resources.  

Reviewing our reliance on commodity industries like Dairy, Beef, Sheep and Forestry in favour of higher value add industries that are less extractive and damaging!  Investing in the next generation of kiwi companies that tackle these problems with for profit and scaleable technologies.
five_things_to_know:
I am a proud Kiwi - living in Hawkes Bay, NZ (a world renowned wine growing, horticulture and farming region) with my beautiful wife and 2 kids.  

When I was in my late 20's I was lucky enough to win a contract to film and present a web series capturing the Great Walks of New Zealand for the Department of Conservation. This life changing experience taught me about Aotearoa New Zealand - the very best of our 3 islands. The floura & fauna, the people, the history & tangata whenua. I also learned that in our relatively unpopulated country, we face huge challenges - economic, conservation and cultural.  

I absorbed and processed this experience over a period of years - travelling the world, building businesses, adventuring through time and space - finally returning to New Zealand in my mid 30's ready to open a new chapter - less focused on "business only" and more focused on making an impact from NZ!

After 12 years as an early stage startup operator and investor with experience growing two $100m+ companies I found myself at a crossroads.  Here is a flavour of my current life:

- Spending lots of time on AfterWork Ventures - investing in the next generation of founders - searching for true outliers and backing them when they're easy to overlook. 

- Developing our beautiful 15 acre lifestyle block, converting the previously unproductive pasture into wetland and rain forest - returning the land to it's natural state.

career_highlights:
My life's work is focusing on the digitization and tokenization of  large food and commodity systems. This is done at the intersection of data, finance and payments.

We are providing financial institutions the tools to describe real world assets, and utilize event-based payment triggering.  Debt and financial obligations are tokenised - creating the building blocks for further downstream securitization.

Data and visibility, as well as persistence of objects on ledger - are powering new structured finance products + capital availability in the agri food space.

These are the themes of both FSCO and Synlian QF Fund.

Dipanwita Das is an award-winning technology entrepreneur and the CEO & Co-founder of Sorcero, which she started with the former co-founder and Executive Director of the MIT Media Lab. The Sorcero Language Intelligence Platform infuses AI with domain expertise to power mission-critical decisions in Life Sciences and Insurance. She has graduated from the leading accelerators for high-growth entrepreneurs, including Plug & Play, Startup Creasphere, and MassChallenge. She was the founder & CEO of 42 Strategies, managing digital transformation projects for Richard Branson’s Virgin Unite, Al Gore’s Climate Reality Project, and Bloomberg Philanthropies. An Atlas Corps Fellow and now a board member, she designed the Global Leadership Lab, training leaders from 60+ countries. She completed the Stanford Graduate School of Business’s Executive Program for Social Entrepreneurship, and earned her M.A. from the Institute for Development Studies at the University of Sussex, and her B.A. from St. Stephen’s College, Delhi.

My global impact goals are to support the integration of understanding into technology used by the key sectors we all rely on in our lives: education, health, and insurance. The infrastructure we all rely on to improve our lives, such as medical diagnosis, public health outcomes, healthcare delivery, protecting properties and lives from accidents, are often run on technology that lags behind what is used in social media, advertising, and other areas that have far less essential roles. The role of understanding, education, and expertise is undervalued in solving global challenges, especially when applying technology, so we seamlessly integrate the two to solve challenges that can't be solved by either alone.

My primary venture, Sorcero, infuses AI with deep domain expertise to support mission-critical decision making in life sciences & insurance. We power the transition to the smart enterprise for key sectors, from supporting smart medical diagnostics to smart construction, applying Language Intelligence to decisions they make every day. I started Sorcero with the former co-founder and Executive Director of the MIT Media Lab, and another EHF Fellow, Richard Graves, and we work globally across North America, Europe, and South Asia. Would love to add NZ to our footprint.

industries_other: Mental health
career_highlights:
I help high-achieving professionals and couples resolve complex trauma, CPTSD, and the impact of sexual and relational trauma on their relationships and lives. My work shifts what talk therapy alone can't shift, drawing on Internal Family Systems, Brainspotting, Relational Life Therapy, and somatic approaches grounded in neuroscience and attachment research. Because of my unique approach, this work takes months, not years.

My path to trauma resolution started with crisis counseling in Africa, Asia, and the Middle East, where I saw firsthand how sexual and relational trauma impacts people's work, relationships, and ability to thrive. I hold an MPH from Johns Hopkins with a focus on social and behavioral health.

I've designed and led wellbeing and sustainable leadership programs globally, including curriculum for the Global Solutions Program at Singularity University and Women's Empowerment and Leadership Development for Democratization with the WLUML network. I continue to speak and give workshops on trauma, relationships, and sustainable leadership for organizations worldwide.
how_im_looking_to_support_the_nz_ecosyst: Mentorship, collaboration, and speaking on trauma resolution, relationships, and post-traumatic growth.

impact_goals_nz: I'm looking to contribute to initiatives around sexual violence response and prevention, coercive control and intimate partner violence, and trauma-informed approaches to care. I'm particularly interested in how body-based trauma resolution can support communities navigating the long-term impacts of childhood and relational trauma.
