five_things_to_know:
Elizabeth has led multi-million-dollar recovery programmes for New Zealand Red Cross and was the Executive Director at the Department of Prime Minister and Cabinet leading work to create a learning legacy from the Canterbury earthquakes. Internationally, Elizabeth has worked for the International Federation of the Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies in the Asia-Pacific region. She is a Winston Churchill fellowship recipient, a Leadership New Zealand alumni, an Edmund Hillary Fellow, co-chair of the New Zealand Chapter of the Australasian Women and Emergencies Network and the co-author of Leading in Disaster Recovery: A Companion through the Chaos.

There’s emergence in emergency. Over the years, we have gathered the wisdom and insights from over 100 crisis leaders and thousands of people affected by disaster and disruption to create products that equip you to do well in tough times. 

Hummingly is the creation of Elizabeth McNaughton and Jolie Wills, a duo who have worked in disasters around the world for more than two decades. The realisation that helping one person, one community, one disaster at a time was no longer enough, lit the spark that became Hummingly. Elizabeth and Jolie set about creating easy to use tools that people, communities and workplaces the world over could access to do well in tough times. Hummingly’s products are now helping thousands of people and organisations around the world.

These proven products are based on Elizabeth and Jolie’s knowledge gained in real disaster and crisis situations. They have gathered wisdom from over 100 crisis leaders, feedback from thousands of disaster survivors, scientific insights from their background in cognitive psychology and a shared passion to prepare the world to do disruption, stress and uncertainty well.

Hummingly’s products are now helping thousands of people and organisations around the world.

Our global impact goal is to have these much needed products and information in the hands of households and organisations to lead and do well through mass disruption, disaster, the impacts of climate change, stress and uncertainty - with a primary focus on Australasia, North America, UK and Europe.

five_things_to_know:
Bio:
Emeline is a co-founder of SpaceBase and International Space Consultants, and an Edmund Hillary Fellow. She is on the Board of Trustees for the 10 to the 9th Foundation and an Associate Editor to New Space Publication (USA).  Emeline was most recently on the executive management team as Chief Impact Officer and Executive Vice President of Operations for Singularity University (SU) prior to moving to New Zealand. 

Emeline has a BS in Physics from the University of the Philippines and a MS in Earth and Space Science from York University, Canada.  She attended the International Space University (ISU) Space Studies Program in Strasbourg, France and subsequently worked and volunteered for ISU developing programs and running academic operations for two decades for its graduate programs around the world.  In commercial space development, she has worked and consulted for several start-ups including serving as Director of Program Development and Research, and Director of Operations for Space Adventures Ltd, a space tourism company sending private citizens to the International Space Station, and Odyssey Moon and Moon Express, which plan to commercialize transport to the Moon. She is an advisor to Ceres Robotics and Extraterrestrial Power. Emeline is co-author of the book “Realizing Tomorrow: The Path to Private Space Flight,” University of Nebraska Press (2013), which was selected that year for the US Air Force’s Chief of Staff Reading List.

SpaceBase
SpaceBase is a not-for-profit LLC with a charitable constitution incorporated in New Zealand (Nov 2017) by three Edmund Hillary Fellows from the U.S. We have extensive expertise and experience in the public and private space industry, NASA, and international space entrepreneurial projects. We also maintain our global networks through the International Space University, Singularity University, and Frontier Development Lab communities. We are currently mentoring several space start-up companies in New Zealand and the U.S.

Long-term goal: Creating Global Entrepreneurial Space Ecosystems
SpaceBase’s mission and vision is to democratise access to space and address the inequality in the global space economy through capacity building in developing and emerging countries. For the past three years, SpaceBase has been prototyping in New Zealand. We have been catalysing and supporting the growth of a national and local entrepreneurial space ecosystem. We do this through educational programs, entrepreneurial initiatives such as mentorship, leveraging incentive prizes to catalyse projects and startups, and community building. We promote collaboration between different stakeholders through platforms and shared projects. Leveraging our lessons-learned, we are now in the best position to educate, train, and advise government and adjacent industry decision makers interested in catalysing their own space industry and ecosystem in a city, region, or country. We identify how stakeholders can leverage the lower costs and increased capabilities of exponential technologies in space to create opportunities to participate in this new commercial space industry. This initiative is a multi-year effort and needs local collaboration to increase the skills and size of a local space workforce in New Zealand and beyond.

Short Term Goals:
SpaceBase is creating a multi-course online training program and consulting service that would give government decision makers or adjacent industry leaders a pathway to understand their opportunities, capabilities, needs, and steps to be able to catalyse a space industry within their local region. A consulting service is available to further advise and assess needs for creating a strategic plan of action customized to a specific local area.

impact_goals_nz:
SpaceBase is currently focused on expanding New Zealand space activities and providing opportunities for New Zealand residents in the growing space industry. We are working with regional governments to grow opportunities for space industry in their regions, encouraging and mentoring new space startups, and working on educational initiatives for capacity building for industry and to open opportunities for young Kiwis.

In five years we hope that any young person in New Zealand can see a path to how they can be involved in space, with thousands of people employed in space projects in New Zealand, and New Zealand spacecraft spanning the solar system.

five_things_to_know:
Co-Founder, SpaceBase and International Space Consultants

Eric Dahlstrom is a co-founder of SpaceBase, a social enterprise focused on democratizing space for everyone by co-creating space ecosystems in emerging countries, starting with New Zealand. Eric works with his wife, Emeline Paat-Dahlstrom, to promote space activities in New Zealand through education, entrepreneurial projects, collaborative community building. Through SpaceBase, they have given more than 100 presentations through New Zealand, helped create several communities supporting space activities, delivered two national space and aerospace prize challenges, and mentored several NZ space companies.

Mr. Dahlstrom is a space engineer, astronomer, and consultant who has worked on spacecraft design and space science for 35 years, both for NASA and the commercial space industry. He studied physics, astronomy, and space systems engineering and is currently a member of the faculty of the International Space University, having taught in ten countries.
 
Mr. Dahlstrom worked for NASA on the design of the International Space Station including the Russian interface. He co-authored NASA’s “Lessons Learned from Challenger” report and the risk of Shuttle accidents. Mr. Dahlstrom has supported a dozen entrepreneurial space companies, served as project manager for commercial lunar spacecraft and payloads, and managed team projects at Singularity University. He is a planetary sciences mentor for the NASA Frontier Development Lab, applying artificial intelligence and satellite data to the hazard of asteroid impact and other global problems.

career_highlights:
In 2009, I founded GLOBAÏA, motivated by my belief in the crucial role that worldviews can play in shaping our present and future existence on Earth. The GLOBAÏA website showcases the evolution of my work over the years, providing a glimpse into my contributions.

By adopting an innovative approach that merges design, art, film, and data with fields such as anthropology, philosophy, and geoscience, I have explored new ways of visualising our past, present, and future. This interdisciplinary method has allowed me to present complex data and ideas in a fresh and insightful manner, promoting a more profound understanding of the Anthropocene and its effects on our planet.

I feel humbled by the recognition my work has received from esteemed organisations like National Geographic and utilised by organisations like the World Economic Forum, the United Nations, the Stockholm Resilience Centre, the Leonardo DiCaprio Foundation, and Netflix. Furthermore, the film Welcome to the Anthropocene, which I co-created with Owen Gaffney, had the honour of opening the second Earth Summit, Rio+20.

I work alongside my team members at Greaterthan and as part of a lively ‘Future of Work’ ecosystem with communities such as Ouishare and Enspiral. 

A key part of our work at Greaterthan is to combine three elements that we believe can create cultures and structures that contribute to more healthy system for all: dynamic leadership, healthy teams and organizations as ecosystems.

career_highlights:
Frédérique Irwin, president and CEO of the National Women's History Museum, has more than 25 years of experience in strategic management, nonprofit and commercial business, operations, impact, and entrepreneurship. 

Prior to joining the Museum, she served as Managing Director of Impact Strategy at the Sorenson Impact Center, where she managed high-level partnerships with leading organizations in the social impact sector.  During that time she also led initiatives around improving equity in higher education and driving systems-level change for equity for women in entrepreneurship. 

She was the founder and CEO of Her Corner, a company educating women on how to scale their businesses, from 2012 until its sale in 2021. Over that period, she expanded Her Corner across multiple cities in the US, was a frequent speaker on the state of entrepreneurship for women in the US, and helped thousands of women-owned businesses scale. 

Frederique has taught entrepreneurship and business at the Kogod School of Business at American University.
how_im_looking_to_support_the_nz_ecosyst: Happy to make professional introductions and connections. Also happy to share my thoughts from a business perspective.
impact_goals_nz:
I would love to work with NZ companies to collaborate and deliver impact projects together or pursue grant opportunities together. 

In addition, Her Corner may still be looking to expand its peer group model in New Zealand. I spent my year in NZ learning about the entrepreneurship ecosystem (and am happy to share what I learned), and I know that while the profile of women business owners is similar to that of the US and Canada, the culture is quite different.
how_to_support_my_work: Let me know if you are also working / leading / running social impact projects in different parts of the world. I would love to know if we can partner together!
five_things_to_know:
Kia Ora, everyone. My name is Frederique, please call me Fred. 

Today I serve as President and CEO of the National Women's History Museum in Wash DC. Previously I served as Managing Director of Impact Strategy for The Sorenson Impact Center where I manage high-level partnerships with leading organizations in the social impact sector. I am constantly looking for opportunities to bring Sorenson's leading data analysis and impact measurement team to meaningful organizations - so that together we can make a difference, while also measuring the impact and telling and teaching these important stories!

I am also the founder of Her Corner Inc., a social impact company I sold in June 2021, that continues to serve women business owners committed to growth in their businesses.

I am passionate about helping women business owners grow their companies and believe all women business owners must see themselves as part of the economic recovery in 2021 and beyond. 

In 2018, my husband and I moved to New Zealand with our 3 children. I spent the year studying the market for women entrepreneurs, meeting with and getting to know New Zealand women business owners, working from co-working spaces to get to know the entrepreneurial landscape, and trying to understand the resources in place for women business owners. We returned to the US in July 2019. 

While living in the US I have spoken on the topic of the state of women and entrepreneurship at The White House (2016), The U.S. State Department, as well as companies such as Marriott, Chobani, Microsoft, AstraZeneca, TD Bank, and others. I have also taught business and entrepreneurship at the Kogod School of Business at American University.  

I am a dual citizen of the US and France. I speak French, Spanish, Russian, and English fluently. I cannot wait for the chance to properly learn te reo, and wish I had learned more while living in NZ.

Graedon Parker (b. 1990) is an award winning graphic artist, entrepreneur and wellbeing / conservation advocate, based in Aotearoa New Zealand.

As an Edmund Hillary Fellow (C3 Kopakopa), Graedon’s creative leadership has been pivotal in the success  of health & wellbeing social enterprise & lifestyle brand Organic Mechanic (OM), and their flagship product - Living Probiotic OM Kombucha, amoungst other organic food & beverages.

With a passion for men's health and initiatory rites of passage, Graedon collaborates with groups in Aotearoa and around the world to create transformational experiences for men of all ages to connect, listen, share and learn through cultural connection, healthy masculine energy, elder wisdom, and physical space where men can lean into their edges and reckon as a collective with what lies within. 

Graedon regularly completes art comissions, websites and branding& identity design work - involved in a small number of organisations with a focus on holistic health & wellbeing, and embodiment the Tiwaiwaka Principles of caring for the whenua (land) as the first priority.

career_highlights:
Founder of Inspiring Stories, a Kiwi charity founded with a vision to back young people to change the world. We work to connect, inspire and support young people taking action to make a difference for our future.

Inspiring Stories is the organisation behind Aotearoa's biggest leadership & innovation summit – Festival for the Future; The Impact Awards – which provides $30K in prizes annually to support Aotearoa's leading young changemakers; and the year-round Future Leaders programme. 

Over the past decade we've had 13,000+ come through our programmes, put more than $2M into scholarship support to increase access to life-changing opportunities for young people, and awarded $100K+ in prizes and funding. 

Awarded Young New Zealander of the Year in 2015.

impact_goals_nz:
Over the next 5 years we're hoping to secure sustainable funding to help scale up our reach and support for young New Zealanders.

This means building on the current programmes. partnerships and support we offer, as well as new developments. Over the next five years we want to:

1. Connect, inspire and support thousands of young New Zealanders to take action and make a difference for our future.

2. Increase access to life-changing opportunities through scholarship support for more young people in Aotearoa's most challenged rural communities.

3. Increase access to awards, funding and pathways for Aotearoa's most promising young changemakers and social entrepreneurs.
how_to_support_my_work: Two key ways that others can help to support our impact here in NZ are through: (1) funding and philanthropy; (2) mentors and speakers as part of our programmes.

4. I'm a father, with two little boys that are always up to mischief
5. I run a charity focused on supporting young people in New Zealand – if you're interested in supporting – hit me up!

career_highlights:
I'm a facilitator, experience designer and systems thinker - based in Te Whanganui a Tara/Wellington.  I was born in London, of English and Welsh ancestry - and have spent most of my adult life between Scotland and Aotearoa New Zealand.  I have also lived in Ireland, Poland and Belarus and worked across Asia and the Middle East.

My work explores ‘connectorship’ as a counter-balance to entrepreneurship in social change processes.I seek to nurture connections, connectivity and whole systems as much as supporting individual ventures and organisations.  Wherever possible, I work with people outdoors - finding this stimulates and develops systems awareness, and enlivens personal connection to the rest of the natural world.

I've spent the last two decades immersed in the world of social innovation and enterprise. I began my career with international NGO Oxfam and subsequently worked on developing the European operation of US-based social business Better World Books.  For the last 10 years I’ve been an independent contractor - with leading social entrepreneurs, enterprising charities and progressive foundations. I’ve also delved into questions of culture and context in social entrepreneurship via a Masters’ thesis and a Churchill Fellowship - and developed skills in coaching and eco-therapy practice.

In NZ, I am an Associate with Toi Āria and the Innovation Unit- and one-quarter of a small collective of impact-led professionals called Pocketknife. I am also part of the Collective Intelligence whānau.  UK-side, I am an international Associate for the Social Enterprise Academy and continue to be closely involved with a fantastic bunch of systems thinkers called The Point People.  I volunteer my time on the leadership group of Adventure Therapy Aotearoa and with Conservation New Zealand.

Of all the roles in my portfolio, my favourite is probably as a Night Guide at Zealandia - aka professional kiwi spotter.

* Navigation support for anyone new to Aotearoa New Zealand

name: Hans Philipp Sueltrop
bio_tagline: Aerospace Technology Explorer. Stretching the limits of aerospace technology to benefit humankind and our environment.
currently_based_in: Christchurch, Canterbury 8011 New Zealand
search_tags: Aerospace, Space, Stratosphere, Technology, Engineering, Science, Exploration, Environment, Climate Change, UAV, Unmanned Aircraft, winter22
career_highlights: Chief Technology Officer at Kea Aerospace, whose mission is to grow the aerospace sector and drive commercial aerospace projects from NZ. He manages their technical team, leading the design, manufacture and testing of a new solar powered High Altitude Pseudo Satellite.
how_im_looking_to_support_the_nz_ecosyst: Connecting people, sharing ideas and experiences.
key_networks:
Aerospace Christchurch
The Explorers Club
impact_goals_nz:
Considering myself as an explorer I love the idea of colonizing Mars and expanding deeper into space. It will create advancements in useful technologies but also provide unimaginable adventures.

Nevertheless, I think we have to sort out our planet Earth first. 

As an aerospace engineer and scientist I am working on solutions to create and provide better data intelligence to benefit better decision making. Aerial intelligence provided by our unique stratospheric unmanned aircraft and tools will help us to monitor our environment, and to save and manage rare resources.

Our NZ environmental agencies and organisations will have access to high quality, up to date aerial data intelligence to make better decisions on things like policies and resource management. Farmers will be able to save water and pesticides while achieving better harvests and optimize their pasture management.
how_to_support_my_work: I tend to get energized from the passion of others around me - so just be yourself but in right in your element...
five_things_to_know:
Our world is changing fast. Thus data and derived intelligence have become the world's most valuable resource.

At Kea Aerospace, we are developing the Kea Atmos, the largest unmanned aircraft in the Southern Hemisphere in order to harvest aerial data from the Stratosphere flying at around 20km altitude. With a wing span of 32m and fully covered in solar panels a Kea Atmos will be able to operate for weeks without landing -  purely on clean energy.

A fleet of Kea Atmos will be continuously operating and carrying different payloads from high resolution multi-spectral cameras to synthetic aperture radars and atmospheric sensors. Flying in the Stratosphere we will operate in a sweet spot for data collection. Our remote sensing data will have much better resolutions/quality than satellites, while our aircraft can further operate extremely flexibly. This will allow us to fill significant data gaps and enable better decision making in areas like environmental monitoring, maritime surveillance, precision agriculture, smart city projects and disaster management.

how_im_looking_to_support_the_nz_ecosyst:
I'm happy to give any guidance I can, to help anyone in the New Zealand ecosystem working on projects in my areas of interest, and additionally help anyone I can with specific market advice and connections. 

Over the years I've gathered some experience structuring projects and exits, so speak to me about these if you think it would help your project.
impact_goals_nz:
An early priority for me is to observe and learn about ways to better balance economic advancement with respecting the environment and the intrinsic worth of people. New Zealand's firm focus on this means the conversations that need to happen worldwide, are happening there. 

I want to see if we can apply this philosophy to businesses, and whether and how far we can disseminate it. I want my contributions to result in quantifiable added employment and the growth of businesses that drive adoption of technology and systems to reduce harm to our planet.
five_things_to_know:
1. I'm deeply concerned about the damage we're doing to the planet's ability to support our species, impending climate-change-led mass displacement of people, and the need for economic models that recognise the intrinsic value of people in an age of automation-driven mass unemployment. 

2. I also see the need for immediate fixes for income insecurity especially in roles that are socially important but that the market does not value. 

3. I'm lucky to have had a range of experiences and some positive outcomes from B2B venture building after a first career as a lawyer.  I've exposure to working capital / alternate finance, logistics in Central and East Africa, use of blockchain in trade management, legal issues concerning blockchain-supported fundraising and hyper-fractionalised asset ownership. I want to contribute these learnings to collaborating to find and back projects that address the above challenges.

4. Current interests:  yield-generation (including from non-tech ventures), clean(er) energy applications, critical raw materials for the battery industry, also exploring models in mining that encourage indigenous participation and benefit. 

5. Life goals: We need to cherish and protect liberal societies that are still functional, where people aren't already divided into camps by predatory special interests; for this reason I'm interested to add my efforts to Aotearoa succeeding. We must slow down the damage we do to the planet so we can buy our kids time to find better solutions to environmental collapse than we've managed to do. We must find the will to build societies and systems that are not predatory of the mass of people.

industries_other: Activism
career_highlights: Co-Founder of Global Health New Zealand, improving global health and lead of Hep C Action Aotearoa, dedicated to the eradication of Hepatitis C in NZ by 2025. Deeply passionate about reforming the criminal justice system and drug law reform.
how_im_looking_to_support_the_nz_ecosyst: Connections to decision makers in several areas, I have legal knowledge that can help other Fellows. We will be hiring staff as we scale up.

impact_goals_nz:
We want to continue to manage projects that see viral hep eliminated in several countries; we want hep C eliminated in Aotearoa and for Hep C Action to run the national awareness campaign for that.
We intend to increase access to medications for emerging economies through collective procurement deals and deliver a voluntary telehealth service to remote and under resourced locations.
how_to_support_my_work: Looking for champions on hep stigma- we need visible people to stand with us and say- "I don't have hepatitis, I have had the test, have you?"
five_things_to_know:
Having narrowly survived chronic hep C in 2016, I turned my attention to the global trading structures that allow such inequity in medication pricing. I became a regular commentator in the media and was able to lead many people to treatment. 
I have launched the international symbol #hepcbutterfly and run national and international awareness campaigns. 

Becoming a Fellow has launched my project internationally. With C6 Fellow Victor Zonana, we have formed Global Health New Zealand, primarily to work on viral hep elimination projects country by country in the Pacific. The first country is Niue, a project funded entirely by Fellows, leading the world. 
The World Health Organisation has set 2030 to eliminate global hepatitis, no country is yet on track- until now. GHNZ is working with the WHO in the Western Pacific Region to widen our model for elimination. 

My public profile is also now linked to human rights. I graduated with a law degree in 2019. During the course of my studies I became aware of New Zealand's legendary jailhouse lawyer, Arthur Taylor, who had taken the cause of prisoner voting to the Supreme Court. It appeared to me that this extraordinary defender of human rights had become essentially a political prisoner. A year of submissions and stories eventually saw him released to my family address on parole in 2019. We appear on various justice matters in the media frequently.

career_highlights:
Founder of Collective Intelligence (CQ), a diverse member organisation for personal and professional development. We create more resilient and effective individuals by bringing together people who think differently.
 
Our new initiative, Impact Teams, is taking a tested methodologies to work on organisational development and improve outcomes.
how_im_looking_to_support_the_nz_ecosyst:
I connect people and curate diverse, impactful groups of people to work on gritty problems using the power of collective intelligence. 

I want to facilitate, grow and develop these connections as part of the outstanding global EHF network that I am now a part of.

Collective Intelligence Team: Half Sugar 

B Lab Australia and New Zealand

B Corp
impact_goals_nz:
I’ve been working on extending an aspect of the people-centric work we do at Collective Intelligence, curating something called Impact Teams. As the name suggests, Impact Teams are targeted at creating lasting, meaningful impact within the organisations they help.

They’re a logical extension of the work I’ve done enhancing the effectiveness of individuals though the open, team-based, radical vulnerability methodology of Collective Intelligence. Impact Teams are built around harnessing the power of cognitive diversity, and are carefully curated teams designed to reveal and unleash the solutions to all sorts of challenging human problems around the dining table, board table, factory floor or farm gate.  

We’re in a beta-testing phase of launching this project and are looking for organisations who have big issues or opportunities, and also a big desire to tackle them collaboratively to create genuine felt impact.  

Intrigued? Talk to me!
how_to_support_my_work:
By having a chat to see where we connect and the energy lies.  I’m an old-fashioned sort of bugger and like to talk - either on the phone or via Zoom .

If I don’t talk to you first, hit me up via Linkedin or my website below.
five_things_to_know:
Our company, Collective Intelligence, provides facilitated, team-based leadership growth and support to professionals who want to adapt, survive and thrive. Our laboratory? A New Zealand-wide community of curious, competent, ambitious and authentic professionals drawn from 70+ industries and professions, who are curated into diverse teams that wield the power of collective intelligence to evolve themselves (and others). 

My experience? Over 25 years of refining a uniquely New Zealand approach of professional development into the peer-to-peer learning model that's unique.

Jade is deeply passionate about design for social impact, community-led collective impact, and co-designing with people of lived experience. She is an advocate for diversity, equity, and inclusion, and in particular, is exploring how these concepts are expressed in an Aoteaora context. Jade is a firm believer in tangata tiriti meaningfully partnering and building relationships with tangata whenua to collaborate on innovative solutions for thriving societies.

Jade is joining the Innovation Unit as Director of Innovation and Partnerships in Aotearoa, New Zealand, with a focus on building meaningful relationships across all levels: from grassroots community, not-for-profit and NGO leaders, through to local and central government changemakers. Her main priority is to enable, enact, and encourage social innovation, social change, and systems change.

Prior to joining Innovation Unit, Jade worked on a myriad of game-changing projects with change-making organisations. She was part of the leadership group at NZ’s largest philanthropic organisation Foundation North, an associate at the Centre for Social Impact, a design thinking lecturer at AUT University, co-founder of the social change creative agency Curative, and chapter host of CreativeMornings in Auckland, where she is still connected to the global community.

Alongside Innovation Unit, Jade currently holds a few governance and advisory roles: HFWSLG (Healthy Families Waitākere Strategic Leadership Group), NACEW (National Advisory Council for Employment of Women) and MECAG (Ministerial Ethnic Community Advisory Group) as well as being a proud mum of one, and a lover of cheese.

impact_goals_nz:
By incorporating positive environmental messages and solutions in games inspired by and designed around nature, its beauty and the adversity the natural world faces, I hope to be able to connect with as many of the 2.5 billion active gamers as I can through my company Tigertron. While I personally watch as many environmental documentaries as I can, I do realize that there is a huge segment of the global populace that consumes entertainment, such as games. 

By approaching these citizens of the world through interactive entertainment, I feel there's an enormous opportunity to effect change through gaming. New Zealand's progressive and thoughtful approach to protecting its own delicate ecosystem is an inspiration in the things I design, and I hope by immersing myself in its culture to be able to create even more effective games, digital art and collaborations. 

TLDR version: Let's make games, digital art installations, multimedia projects, and anything that can interact with future people in a digital age.
how_to_support_my_work:
Looking for people to collaborate with in the interactive entertainment space (video games, interactive art and media installations), with a focus on raising awareness of critical, global environmental challenges. Advice and introductions to environmentally-minded, tech savvy persons who want to brainstorm new ways of connecting and informing people on how they can help the planet is my goal. Learning more about the history of New Zealand, its natural ecosystem, and its local and global environmental initiatives is a top priority for me. 

Come to me with ideas of how I can help you gameify your own ideas into something various audiences (from young to adult) can enjoy, or bring your expertise to my projects to collaborate on and enrich them in ways I haven't yet imagined.
five_things_to_know: Former games journalist and frequent writer and contributor to websites, magazines, and books specialized in the game industry. My goal is to bring the very disparate worlds of environmentalism and video game entertainment together, combining environmental themes with cutting edge games to be enjoyed by (hopefully) millions of gamers around the world.

five_things_to_know: James is a French and British marine scientist, filmmaker and consultant, originally from the French Alps near Geneva. He holds a MSc in Marine Systems and Policies from the University of Edinburgh and a Bachelor of Arts in Film from the University of Exeter. For nearly a decade, he has worked globally as a consultant in ocean science and policy communications, on issues of high seas governance, microplastics pollution, climate change impacts on the ocean, deep reef research, ocean risk and marine protected area management. He is the co-founder and CEO of Manaia Productions, a strategic communications and film production agency specialized in nature conservation, science and livelihoods. James has been working for several years as project manager and producer with the International Union for Conservation of Nature, and has worked with WWF, Pew Bertarelli Ocean Legacy, the Ocean Acidification Alliance and the UK government. In 2019, he worked as Media Advisor for the Intergovernmental Oceanographic Commission of UNESCO, in the context of the UN Decade of Ocean Science for sustainable Development. Since 2015 he has been a member of IUCN’s World Commission on Protected Areas, contributing to its marine task force. He lives in Christchurch, New Zealand and is a Fellow with the Edmund Hillary Fellowship. Recently he founded Blue Cradle, a non-profit working in New Zealand promoting ocean conservation and literacy.

industries: Cleantech, Environment, Information and Communications Technology
career_highlights:
I'm on a mission to leave the planet in better shape than when I arrived. One of the ways I'll achieve this is through my climate tech startup www.CarbonClick.com which I co-founded in 2018. CarbonClick is a multi-purpose climate action platform that helps individuals and businesses take climate action by engaging in the world's highest-quality carbon credits. 

Individuals can calculate their carbon footprint, get a personalised list of footprint reduction actions they can commit to and finally, contribute towards climate projects which further reduce their impact. 

Businesses can accelerate their 'Net Zero' journeys through a number of offset programmes, such as bundling carbon offsets with product/services, engaging their consumers at the checkout, or running an in-house programme for their staff.
how_im_looking_to_support_the_nz_ecosyst: I want to support others on "team planet" - helping entrepreneurs and like-minded individuals on their journey. We're also excited to grow a team of hard-working and passionate climate warriors to help the company achieve its mission.
key_networks: Impact hub - Generation Impact Fellowship
impact_goals_nz:
We are on an aggressive growth mission to expand CarbonClick globally by adding value to businesses and forming strategic partnerships. 

In 5 years, we will be "the offset platform" of choice for the global carbon offset market, enabling millions of businesses and individuals to take climate action, funding thousands of climate projects that help reverse climate change.
how_to_support_my_work:
The planet can't wait... we need to invest, at scale, into thousands of solutions to urgently address climate change.

If anyone wishes to support our specific venture, we'd be humbled. We need help growing CarbonClick into hundreds of businesses - giving us access to tens of millions of consumers.
five_things_to_know:
I was born in Norway to Polish parents, but I never felt quite Norwegian nor Polish. I owe it to my parents for setting me off on an international journey, which started when I attended an international primary school, and culminated in a 6-month backpacking trip around Southeast Asia followed by a stint in London for 4 years. Just as we felt done with London and were off to Norway, we embarked on another 6-month trip that included New Zealand... and (what is quite common among NZ travellers) we never left!

After 6 years working at various corporates in NZ, I decided to pursue a purpose-driven startup addressing one of the greatest challenges in the history of mankind - climate change. CarbonClick was formed when I saw first-hand how difficult it is for businesses to create engaging, trustworthy carbon offset solutions. 

We've created an enterprise-grade carbon offsetting platform that can scale to make a measurable impact in the fight against climate change. We're now on the scaling stage of our journey - exciting times ahead!

five_things_to_know:
I'm a serial social entrepreneur with a passion for positive social change.  My work crosses a range of sectors, but my role is similar within each - develop a business/inititative/project that will create positive social change. 

I founded Take My Hands to find a way to connect health providers in the Pacific crying out for good equipment, with the great equipment we in New Zealand will no longer use despite it being in good working order.  Over the last five years we've sent over 130 tonne of good working equipment to 9 countries where it will be used to help hundreds of thousands of people over the next five to 10 years.  By tapping into spare capacity in transport and logistics we've been able to do that for under $100K 

I co-founded a collective impact initiative in West Auckland NZ that has built a model of cross sector collaboration that is keeping our most at risk and vulnerable young people engaged in education.  My role here has been identifying need and then building the frameworks and working with the agencies and organisations to facilitate their collaboration in delivering a solution to that need.  www.aawt.org is where you'll find more and from May 2021 a report covering the last 5 years of work. 

Pacific Clean I founded in response to the need for improved infection control in the the Pacific.  By supplying great cleaning products to NZ partners we are able to send an effective cleaner that kills bacteria and viruses to the Pacific contributing to improved infection control.

bio_tagline: Hauora Aotearoa teaches daily wellbeing practices so our teachers and youth can live well, learn well and lead well.

career_highlights:
He uri ahau nō Ngāti Apa, nō Ngāti Ruanui, nō Ngāti Tūwharetoa. Ko Jase Te Patu tōku ingoa.

I have worn many hats in my life — New Zealand sportsman, Les Mills choreographer and trainer, international performer, radio host, TV presenter, master facilitator, international speaker, and now Founder and CEO of a school-based mental health charity. Each role has shaped my journey and led me to where I am today.

I’ve also known the heartbreak of losing loved ones to suicide and witnessed the ripple effects of mental distress in our communities. These experiences sparked my calling to step up and do my part to make a difference.

For over 32 years, I’ve shared tools for wellbeing in every form possible. In 2018, I began bringing mindfulness, movement, and Māori pūrākau into ECEs and schools through M3 Mindfulness. These tools empower tamariki with self-awareness, resilience, and the wisdom of their own culture.

Now, through Hauora Aotearoa, we’re building on that foundation — supporting rangatahi with a holistic, culturally grounded approach to mental health. With the guidance of Tā Mason Durie, who has encouraged us to “live well, learn well, and lead well,” our vision is to create generational change — uplifting school communities to thrive, not just survive.

Beyond my mahi in schools, I’m passionate about facilitating Mental Health First Aid for adults and youth. It’s a privilege to empower workplaces and communities with the skills to recognise and respond to mental health needs, creating safer, more supportive environments for all.

As an international speaker, MC, and facilitator, I’m driven to share messages of hope, resilience, and wellbeing with audiences worldwide. Whether in schools or on stage, my mission remains the same — to equip our people with the tools to live healthier, happier lives across Aotearoa.
how_im_looking_to_support_the_nz_ecosyst: Hauora Aotearoa supports the Aotearoa ecosystem through whānaungatanga. We work with school communities, from the learners to teachers and whānau members. Our hope is that by improving the mental health of a school community, we will also be improving the mental health of whole communities, thus lifting the mana and connectedness of a nation.
key_networks:
- Wellbeing industry
- School communities (Kāhui Ako and the Principal's Association)
- Some iwi throughout Aotearoa, particularly Ngāti Apa and Ngāti Ruanui
impact_goals_nz: Through sharing daily wellbeing practices in schools, we aim to reduce suicide rates in NZ, re-engage youth in schools again, and lower the absentee rates.
how_to_support_my_work: We are always looking for Philanthropic funding and volunteers. You can donate to Hauora Aotearoa like many of our Fellows here >>> hauoraaotearoa.com/donate
five_things_to_know:
Maranga mai (wake up). The time is now, e te iwi!
Me mahi tahi tātou hei oranga mō te katoa
~
We must work together for the wellbeing of all. Are you with us?
Our points of difference at Hauora Aotearoa:
- We make the well-known concepts of Te Whare Tapa Whā accessible so our students and teachers can turn the knowledge into everyday wellbeing practices.
- The rangatahi (youth) get to be rangatira (leaders), teaching the wellbeing practices they have learnt to preschoolers, fostering a Tuakana/Teina (big person/little person) relationship.
- We work with teachers, not just the students, so they also build their own wellbeing tools.
- Our kaupapa (programme) comes with 15 NCEA Level 3 Credits as part of the course.
anything_else_to_share: I am on a continuous journey of reclaiming the language that my grandparents and parents were not allowed to speak growing up. Everything I do, including work on our Hauora Aotearoa programme, is infused with reo Māori and the rich teachings and philosophies that make us a unique indigenous culture - one that is celebrated worldwide!

career_highlights: Scientist and Professor of Ophthalmology at the University of Pennsylvania, she holds a PhD and MD with a career has been focus on using gene therapy to cure blindness. She led the clinical trials of the first FDA-approved gene therapy drug in the US and the first to be approved worldwide. The drug Luxturna is now used to treat a congenital blindness caused by mutations in both children and adults.
how_im_looking_to_support_the_nz_ecosyst: We will be connecting experts around the world with those in New Zealand to expedite progress in science, gene therapy drug development, clinical care, test procedures and business. We will hire and train staff and mentor trainees at all levels and in a range of fields.
key_networks: American Association of Physicians, National Academy of Medicine, American Academy of Arts and Sciences, National Academy of Science, American Society for Cell and Gene Therapy, Association for Research in Vision and Ophthalmology, Foundation Fighting Blindness and the RD Fund, Retina International, Research to Prevent Blindness, Fight for Sight, Opus Genetics, Choroideremia Research Foundation, RDH12 Fund for Sight. I serve on government scientific committees for the National Institutes of Health/National Eye Institute and am the co-founder of several biotechnology companies.
impact_goals_nz:
We are driven to improve the lives of people in New Zealand and around the world by addressing severe vision loss. By pooling our complementary expertise in science, visual arts, and management, we believe that we can make a positive and lasting impact on individuals, families, the health system and society by initiating an effort to combat severe, debilitating and hereditary blindness. Our work in New Zealand, which will focus on a congenital blinding disease, could serve as a stepping stone to helping afflicted individuals in the rest of the world.

In our first year in NZ (pending Covid-19-related travel restrictions), we will establish the details of implementation of a retinal gene therapy program. We will address regulatory issues, identify physical space, develop standard operating procedures, and work to guide implementation of novel virtual reality test protocols. We will ship reagents and any necessary equipment. We will initiate the research studies, including identifying and organizing site(s), engaging collaborators, hiring and training staff, and setting up the test procedures. By the end of the Fellowship, we aim to have sufficient data to test a new therapy and to know whether this new therapy for inherited blindness is likely to be effective. If all goes well, we will then take the next steps to seek drug approval.
how_to_support_my_work: We look forward to networking and collaborating with other EHF Fellows, scientists, clinicians, patients, and entrepreneurs both locally and internationally.
five_things_to_know: I am a physician-scientist with experience/expertise in molecular biology, vector development and gene therapy translational studies. I have developed gene transfer approaches to test treatment strategies for retinal degenerative and ocular neovascular diseases, to elucidate retinal differentiation pathways, and to identify pathogenetic mechanisms that lead to blindness. I have established a true "from bench to bedside" program, and thus am familiar with steps necessary to go from proof-of-concept all the way to those prerequisite for drug approval. I was the scientific leader of a team that translated reversal of blindness in animal models to demonstration of efficacy and safety in children and adults. My team achieved many firsts along the way: the first to enroll pediatric subjects with a non-lethal disease as gene therapy participants, development of procedures with which to accurately and stably deliver genes to retinal cells, and development of a novel outcome measure to assess vision. This work led to the first approved gene therapy for inherited disease by the USA Food and Drug Administration (FDA, 2017) by the European EMA (European Medicines Agency, 2018) and now around the world. Through my Center, the Center for Advanced Retinal and Ocular Therapeutics (CAROT) at the University of Pennsylvania Perelman School of Medicine, through companies that I have founded or advised, and through a network of patient-oriented foundations, I plan to continue to tackle some of the limitations of current gene therapy technologies and to develop gene-based therapies for currently untreatable diseases. I also plan to continue to serve as an interface between the laboratory the clinic, and biotechnology companies, and to mentor scientists and physicians at all career stages.

five_things_to_know:
My work, in old projects and new, builds relationships across socio-economic barriers to support people’s development in collaboration, to amplify under-heard voices and unheard stories, and to encourage avenues of mutual aid that bring about mutual freedom. Seeing those I care about act boldly to bring about the more beautiful world their hearts know is possible lights me up. I'm here and keen for the work of capacity development, walking alongside people as they take their next most elegant step for themselves and those beyond them. I also get great joy from that moment when – in musical improvisation (a jam) for example – everyone synchronously changes what they are doing and it just works, harmoniously... like the music is playing you, playing us. I'm keen for more people to see this and to experience it more myself. 

I began collaborating with Phillip Barker and Glen Lauder in 2017 as a technology lead and practice partner for a social and environmental fellowship composed of community-based leaders working across communities and institutions in Aotearoa. I helped design and deliver a series of wānanga and exercises, and together, we developed “practice profiles”, a participatory evaluation and reporting methodology that preserves the integrity and value of fellows’ practices and reflections.   
With the commitment that their work not be colonised nor colonising, since 2009, Glen and Phillip have been open sourcing their work, entrusting it to the Maranui Institute. It’s purpose is to guide people into right relationship with the land and her peoples – in connection to Te Whanganui a Tara Wellington. Linked to a wide field of NZ and global practitioners, I have joined the institute to conduct Participatory action research that deepens collaborative relationships towards sharing assets and even power.

At Loomio, an open source communication and decision-making platform, I’m an co-owner and head of ‘group success’. Loomio serves 10,000+ monthly users globally, and our sustainability challenges call for improved marketing and helping more customers succeed with Loomio. My role in these challenges is to help groups have conversations that lead to clear outcomes and, ultimately, work better together with Loomio.

A keyword map of my Why/ How/ What in 2019 October: https://www.dropbox.com/s/a9izur6i1l5xxuj/points-of-unity_personal.svg?dl=0

Just starting to write publicly at https://medium.com/@coopchange
new publication coming soon (as of 10/10/2020)

career_highlights: Co-founder of Hummingly, which takes learnings from disaster to help people and organisations grow from times of stress and uncertainty. They develop products, such as the “Resilience Genie” that supports wellbeing and resilience at work or the “Cards for Calamity” that help people process challenging life events.
how_im_looking_to_support_the_nz_ecosyst: We are well connected through various sectors across New Zealand and are open to likeminded people and businesses to share ideas, knowledge and to collaborate with.

impact_goals_nz: Supporting organisations and individuals to lead and do well through change, stress and uncertainty - through delivering workshops, video content and analogue knowledge products (card sets). Change, uncertainty, large scale disasters will only increase as climate change accelerates. we are here to prepare people for this reality.
how_to_support_my_work: Support us to spread the word so we can connect the pressure-tested tools and knowledge that the world needs right now, with individuals and organisations who can benefit.
five_things_to_know:
Jolie has a Masters in Cognitive Psychology and is a leading psychosocial expert in disaster and disruption. Jolie has studied how the mind works under prolonged pressure, how we make decisions and how our reactions and behaviours are impacted by stress. Jolie is a survivor of the Christchurch earthquake and has lived disaster recovery with her family first-hand. She developed services and training to help her fellow citizens for the Red Cross, local government and academia. Jolie is a Winston Churchill fellowship recipient, a Leadership New Zealand alumni, an Edmund Hillary Fellow and the co-author of Leading in Disaster Recovery: A Companion through the Chaos, primary author of the New Zealand Psychological First Aid guide and is Co-Chair of the New Zealand chapter of Australasian Women in Emergencies Network.

There’s emergence in emergency. Over the years, we have gathered the wisdom and insights from over 100 crisis leaders and thousands of people affected by disaster and disruption to create products that equip you to do well in tough times.

Hummingly is the creation of Elizabeth McNaughton and Jolie Wills, a duo who have worked in disasters around the world for more than two decades. The realisation that helping one person, one community, one disaster at a time was no longer enough, lit the spark that became Hummingly. Elizabeth and Jolie set about creating easy to use tools that people, communities and workplaces the world over could access to do well in tough times. Hummingly’s products are now helping thousands of people and organisations around the world.

These proven products are based on Elizabeth and Jolie’s knowledge gained in real disaster and crisis situations. They have gathered wisdom from over 100 crisis leaders, feedback from thousands of disaster survivors, scientific insights from their background in cognitive psychology and a shared passion to prepare the world to do disruption, stress and uncertainty well.

Hummingly’s products are now helping thousands of people and organisations around the world.

Our global impact goals are to get this knowledge into the hands of every household and organisation that needs support with mass disruption, stress and uncertainty, with a focus on Australasia, North America, US and Europe.

career_highlights:
I am the founding CEO of HeritageSG - a subsidiary of the National Heritage Board (NHB), a statutory board under the Ministry of Culture, Community, and Youth (MCCY). HeritageSG delivers innovative museum and heritage-related services by providing immersive experiences and supporting sector-wide initiatives in Singapore and beyond. HeritageSG’s projects include marquee events such as Singapore Heritage Festival and Singapore Night Festival, which together attract more than 2 million visitors annually. Children’s Museum of Singapore is an integral part of HeritageSG, laying the foundation for our future heritage. Additionally, HeritageSG explores archaeological services, traveling exhibitions, digital services, and brand partnerships. HeritageSG’s digital services team provides interactive museum visitor experiences such as virtual reality and interactive displays.

Most recently, I was Chief Executive Officer of Fintopia Indonesia, the largest micro lending digital platform in Indonesia, serving millions of underbanked and unbanked, and the Chief Strategy Officer of Fintopia Singapore. Before joining Fintopia, I held the position of Regional Head of Government Partnerships and Engagement at Shopify and was the Country Head of Google for Education Next Billion Users (NBU) initiative in Indonesia. In addition, I was the Executive Director of the Lien Centre for Social Innovation at Singapore Management University.

Entrepreneurship is at the core of my professional journey. I've co-founded and advised numerous start-ups across various sectors globally, receiving funding from investors such as Y-Combinator and 500 Startups. As a global speaker and media commentator, I've travelled all over the world to deliver lectures and talks and have contributed articles to Channel NewsAsia. I was honoured to receive the European Foundation for Management Development (EFMD) Case Writing Award for my research on an innovative social enterprise in Bali.

I hold degrees from UC Berkeley, Columbia, Stanford Graduate School of Business, and Harvard Kennedy School of Government, and earned my doctorate from the University of Pennsylvania.

bio_tagline: Co-founder of Happen Films, an Aotearoa New Zealand-based documentary filmmaking team.

Christchurch Akaroa Rd Little River, Canterbury 7591 New Zealand
Christchurch, Canterbury 8011 New Zealand
search_tags: documentary, filmmaking, film, video, permaculture, conservation, sustainability, regeneration, Oct22
industries_other: Films
career_highlights: Co-founder and co-director of Happen Films Ltd, producing films that connect us to solutions to our challenges. Recent releases include “Fools & Dreamers: Regenerating a Native Forest” to inspire more regeneration projects and “Living the Change: Inspiring Stories for a Sustainable Future”.
how_im_looking_to_support_the_nz_ecosyst: By sharing through film the stories of people and projects showcasing ways to live more lightly on earth. In this way we're able to support that work and help spread its message and inspiration. Nearly all of our films are free to view online, in order to create as few barriers as possible to sharing these high-impact stories.
impact_goals_nz:
For five years, we have steadily grown both our audience and our reputation for making high-quality, solutions-focused films that inspire people to ‘live the change’. We know from our own experience, however, that it can be hard to turn enthusiasm into action in a world that is, paradoxically, under-resourced for living lightly and creating a fair share for all.

One of the questions driving us right now is, What resources can we contribute along with our films that will ensure our audience members become not just enthusiasts but activists for a healthier, more resilient planet and people? Our intention is to generate or collaborate on resources as part of the release of our films that help our audience to reduce the barriers to living lightly on planet earth.

In addition, we’ve seen the need to broaden our reach so that our films impact and resonate with people beyond our white, middleclass, able-bodied, privileged perspective. Here in Aotearoa New Zealand we live on lands dishonestly colonised, within a culture that is fundamentally racist. 

Another question driving us is, How can we ensure our films contribute to healing both our culture and our planet? First we must reeducate ourselves, which we're doing by learning te reo and tikanga Maori, exploring how we can be good and productive Treaty of Waitangi/Te Tiriti o Waitangi partners, and ensuring diversity exists in our film content and in the people we work with to make our films happen.
how_to_support_my_work:
- By sharing our films widely.
- By letting us know about potential important stories to tell.
- By helping us to form connections within the regeneration/sustainability/community resilience, social justice etc. spaces.
- With funding, from a small one-off donation to financing a whole film. 

Information about how we operate can be found on our website.
five_things_to_know:
I'm the co-founder of Happen Films (with EHF fellow Antoinette Wilson). We're an Aotearoa New Zealand-based documentary filmmaking team.

We are impact storytellers.

We tell stories that help guide us back into harmony with the rest of nature; that inspire people to share, discuss, and develop healing ways of living; that educate, support, and empower people to take the next step towards living more lightly on earth.

This is a journey we're on ourselves and so we can share these stories with genuine enthusiasm and passion.

search_tags: climate action, arts, systems change, Oct22
career_highlights: Visual artist and photographer blending fine-art and technology with a focus on creating large scale art installations that bring remote areas of the planet into urban environments. Installations 'Voices for the Future and 'Antarctica: While You Were Sleeping' were projected on prominent buildings around the world including the UN building in New York. Current project, 'Raised up Sky, is an augmented reality artwork that explores the interconnectivity of humans and nature through the digital scanning and representation of significant trees from the Amazonian and New Zealand forests.
five_things_to_know:
I’m a visual artist based in Auckland, New Zealand. I create large scale art installations that bring remote areas of the planet into urban environments.

In 2019, I created"Voices for the Future," an installation that lit up the United Nations General Assembly and Secretariat buildings in New York ahead of the UN’s Climate Action Summit and global school strikes. The artwork showcased powerful visuals of an iceberg slowly crashing down the sides of the 500-foot tall United Nations building, followed by the voices of the six young advocates addressing their hopes and fears for the future, in relation to the climate crisis.

Currently working on "Amazon - Raised Up Sky," an augmented reality artwork that explores the interconnectivity of humans and nature, a ground-breaking fusion of art and science placing the viewer in the heart of the Amazonian rainforest. Through conversations with scientists, artists and indigenous leaders we explore our physical, biological, and cultural link to trees.

I earned a Master's degree in Environmental Management from the Yale School of Forestry and Environmental Studies, where I explored the role of youth photography in building community and youth agency. Over the past 20 years, I have collaborated on community-based media projects with youth, teachers, neighborhood groups, and nonprofit organizations in places ranging from New Delhi to Nigeria. I co-founded VOICES Inc., a Tucson nonprofit that hired and trained low-income youth to document and share untold community stories. In 2006, I co-founded Finding Voice, a program supporting the literacy development, civic engagement and creative expression of refugee and immigrant students. I am the founding director of CommunityShare, an education initiative focused on re-imagining the relationship between schools and communities.

career_highlights:
For 7 years I lived and worked in rural Sri Lanka, founding a social enterprise that provided training and flexible employment to mothers living on Sri Lanka's tea estates. We existed to find solutions to the complex problems experienced by women in that place, alongside modelling an alternative to the growth-focused and heavily polluting garment industry that surrounded our workshop. We built island-wide supply chains to source food waste that we transformed into non-toxic natural textile dyes and handwoven natural fabrics with an aim of preserving a rapidly dying craft. 

Most of my time is now spent working with communities to identify and map the context-specific challenges they face. Facilitating workshops that engage ecological design thinking practices to co-design solutions to complex wicked problems. 

Alongside this, my focus is on developing localised regenerative fiber systems and exploring what a fashion commons could look like in a UK context.

Textiles - working to develop localised fiber systems in the UK and exploring what a fashion commons looks like. Particular interest in wool and linen. 

I ran a social enterprise in Sri Lanka for 7 years, providing employment and training to mothers living on Sri Lanka's tea estates. We built supply chains across the island to source food waste that we transformed into natural non-toxic textile dyes.

five_things_to_know:
Justin Milano is a serial entrepreneur, executive coach, and the CEO of Abroad.io. He serves by helping influential leaders and their teams actualize their potential through a balance of personal and professional development.  

Abroad.io is a Human Transformation Platform™ that combines coaching, education, and research to improve leadership, team, and portfolio potential. Our offerings are powered by evidenced-based research, practical applications, ancient wisdom, and mindfulness-based practices. 

In the first phase of our mission, Abroad is working with global leadership networks, venture portfolios, innovative organizations, and forward-thinking nations to holistically develop leaders and usher forth the #WellbeingEconomy for the benefit of all.

Justin is a sought after public speaker and has given talks at top universities (e.g. Stanford, Northwestern), venture capital funds (e.g Insight Venture Partners, 500 Startups), and financial institutions (e.g. Singapore Stock Exchange) on topics ranging from high-impact leadership to executive wellbeing.

In the coming months, Justin and the Abroad team will be launching the Innovator Wellbeing Index™, the largest study ever conducted on entrepreneur mental and emotional health to help courageous innovators operate with greater balance and resilience.

In 2019 Justin was selected for the Edmund Hillary Fellowship, an innovative New Zealand government program that looks to attract top social impact entrepreneurs to incubate solutions to global problems in New Zealand.

DSIL Global (Designing for Systems Innovation and Leadership) is an innovation company that brings different parts of society and varying sectors together so that they may better understand and build innovation. From our team to the actors in the projects we take on, we are diverse on purpose because the solutions to the ever-increasing, interdependent, and complex challenges we all face lie there. Diversity ensures we can listen and see the world through vast sets of experiences that create our realities. Inviting in those lenses expands creative thinking far outside of the box! Welcome to the pathway to redesign the world. We pay close attention to context and include the humans experiencing the challenges whatever the project is before us.
Because Leadership plays such a big role in every element of society, we bring thought leadership to this space as well and have a community of 270+ Executives from around the world who have joined our 8-day intensive Executive Course to change their way of doing, and being to create real impact. career_highlights: Joined Square in 2013, when the US financial services and mobile payment company had just 300 employees. A few years later, co-founded Ladies Who Launch as an initiative inside of Square after hearing that so many women entrepreneurs and small business owners yearned for connection and education.  Ladies Who Launch later struck out on its own and is now a fully accredited 501(c)3 organization supporting and funding women entrepreneurs globally.
how_im_looking_to_support_the_nz_ecosyst: I would love anyone who is interesting in supporting, sponsoring, mentoring and/or funding women entrepreneurs to reach out for a chat on how we can get you involved in our work to ensure these incredible women have a fighting chance at bringing their ventures, ideas, businesses and products to life.
impact_goals_nz:
The funding stats for women entrepreneurs are nothing short of abysmal, and when you drill down further for BIPOC/LGBTQ+ entrepreneurs, the numbers are simply diabolical. We must change the funding landscape in order to change the success metrics and outcomes for these women. 

My intent is to tap into new networks of potential mentors and funders in New Zealand and beyond and create a pay it forward ecosystem of funding for women entrepreneurs to thrive, ensuring main streets in Aotearoa and around the world are bustling with these businesses, supporting communities and families, and changing the game for women.
five_things_to_know:
Kia ora, EHF fellows! I'm the Co-Founder ofLadies Who Launch.

Ladies Who Launch is a fully accredited 501(c)3 non profit organization, founded on empowering, connecting and supporting women throughout their entrepreneurial journeys.

Through experiential events and our digital platforms, Ladies Who Launch gives women an unfair advantage to launch and grow successful companies by connecting them with other women entrepreneurs and the capital community.

Until March of 2020, I was the Director of Global Events at Square, a financial services company on a mission to provide economic empowerment to small business owners and entrepreneurs. There, I led a team focused on dreaming up unique and meaningful experiences for Square's customers and employees around the world. During my seven year tenure at Square, I also held a variety of positions across the Experience and Company Culture spectrum, leading Culinary Operations, Talent Programs and Sustainability efforts.

Prior to joining Square, I began my career at the Natural Resources Defense Council, an international environmental non-profit organization where I had the privilege of wearing many hats in the fight for our earth's precious resources for over five years. It was through my fundraising and advocacy work at NRDC that I became ever more impassioned in the conviction that we must work together to save our lands, oceans and wildlife for future generations to enjoy.

I hold a BS in Political Science with a minor in Environmental Studies from California Polytechnic State University, San Luis Obispo and currently reside in Seattle, Washington with my husband, Trevor (also an EHF fellow (Cohort 7)!). We're looking forward to eventually making our way over to Queenstown (where we own a little slice of heaven at the base of the Remarkables) and hosting many EHF gatherings in the future.

five_things_to_know:
T-Guard - Intelligent Driver Management Solution
T-Guard is the leading driver management solution designed to save lives and reduce road accidents with intelligent artificial intelligence (AI) enhanced fleet management so poor driving habits do not cause irreparable damage to families, businesses and properties. Telematics sensors gather data from various devices and multiple datapoints for fleet managers to conduct extensive and in-depth driving behaviour review and analysis, thereby you to inculcate your drivers with safe driving practices.

Our innovative technology alerts drivers and fleet managers when drivers are under-performing due to fatigue and distractions so you will be able to intervene as necessary to ensure road safety. Advanced road warning systems installed on your fleet vehicles alert drivers of potential collisions or lane departure to improve safe driving standards.

T-Guard is created to prevent the following erratic driving behaviours:
-Falling asleep while driving long distances
-Fidgeting with handphones or other distractions while driving
-Failing to keep a proper look out on the road
-Abrupt braking and uncontrolled acceleration which may cause grievous injuries to passengers onboard
-Changing lanes without due care causing unintended accidents
-Tailgating vehicles in front with no regard for safe following distance

International trade is fundamentally important to the recovery and growth of the New Zealand economy.  I have been advocating for the establishment of the Business Growth Fund in New Zealand. It would provide access to expansion capital for ambitious Small and Medium sized Enterprises that are currently underserved by existing financial products. 

The Business Growth Fund would contribute to accelerating the New Zealand economy through long-term strategic investment in export-focused New Zealand SMEs that can contribute to building a productive, sustainable and inclusive New Zealand economy.

As a sixth generation New Zealander whose ancestors settled at Rangihoua Bay, Marsden Cross in 1820, deep connection and commitment to the advancement of New Zealand informs my work and life. 

I spent five years of my childhood in France where my father worked for the New Zealand Embassy and OECD.  I am bilingual in French and have studied German and Japanese.  

My background is in developing businesses internationally throughout the Asia Pacific region, across a range of industries, from SMEs to MNC’s including founding my own export business which I ran for 8 years. These experiences help me appreciate the unique challenges faced by SMEs and startup founders who are invariably constrained by limited resources.  

Since 2014 I have been an active angel investor focusing on investing in startups growing sustainable global businesses. 

Encouraging and empowering women entrepreneurs to raise capital via Icehouse Ventures, Arc Angels and Coralus (formerly SheEO) is also an area I am deeply committed to.

Medical device engineer turned corporate sustainability specialist focused on carbon reduction and climate risk. I combine a decade of design experience with a commensurate interest in international development. I bring industry expertise and first-hand knowledge of Pacific Island clinical conditions to the Fellowship. 

My 2019 thesis in medical device engineering examined the current system for donating used medical equipment into low-resource settings such as the Pacific Islands.  From this research, I identified the need for a system to provide training manuals and asset registers for hospital staff, working with ministries of health, donors and clinicians.  

I responded to this need by developing a business plan to deliver such a system to address existing shortcomings and improve health outcomes. It was entered into the University of Auckland’s Velocity Challenge and won admission to the University’s VentureLab business incubator, with seed capital totaling $5,000. From here, I co-founded the social enterprise Pacific Med Tech to put these ideas into practice. 

I have been based in Auckland NZ since 2015.

name: Laura Dawn Murphy
bio_tagline: Laura Dawn is the Founder, CEO, & Chief Creative at ART NOT WAR. An 18 year veteran of creating and directing social impact campaigns, Laura is a writer, director, political / cultural / digital strategist, and producer of social justice media and campaigns that have reached over 1.5 billion worldwide--40 million in 2020 alone. Laura was the co-creator of Bush in 30 Seconds (2013), the first user generated political ad campaign, a groundbreaking campaign now taught in advertising & communications textbooks across the world.  In 2003, Laura became the founding Cultural & Creative Director of MoveOn.org (she spent a decade helping grow the organization from 750,000 to 8 million members) and as current founder and Chief Creative Officer of award winning cultural strategy agency ART NOT WAR, Laura has spent the last 18 years directing groundbreaking cultural campaigns, innovating digital strategy, and making high impact media that has changed linguistic frameworks, influenced elections, and helped pass progressive legislation. Laura is also the co-founder of the Emily’s List Creative Council and serves on the Advisory Boards of the Climate Mobilization, The Hometown Project, Front Page Live and Swayable, a progressive video testing platform. Her dream is to move the world towards more equity, sustainability, and non violence through data driven creative.
